blocks.text:
Creation
(1) Both A and P manifest assent to work together
(2) A agrees to work for P’s benefit, and
(3) A agrees to work subject to P’s control

Principal
•
Typically, an employer
•
Controls objective but not means

Agent
•
Corporation: Directors, officers, employees
•
Partnership: Partners or employees

Independent Contractor
•
Can be an A if physical conduct subject to P's control
LIABILITY OF PRINCIPAL AND AGENT TO OTHERS
UNDERLYING CAUSE OF ACTION: BREACH OF CONTRACT

Principal’s Contractual Liability
•
May be liable for K entered into by A if A had actual or apparent authority

Express Actual Authority
•
Created through oral or written words

Implied Actual Authority
•
Based on A’s rx understanding of P’s objective

Implied by Position
•
Based on A’s job title or position

Apparent Authority
•
P causes 3P’s rx belief that A has authority to act

Third Party’s Reasonable Belief
•
Based on trade customs, industry standards, and A’s position

blocks.text:
Ratification
(1) P expressly or impliedly demonstrates assent to K
(2) Timely
(3) Parties have capacity, and
(4) P knows material facts

Estoppel
(1) P makes false representation or conceals material fact
(2) With intent to create 3P’s reliance
(3) 3P relies, and
(4) Reliance rxly based on P’s false representation

Disclosed Principal
•    3P knows A is working for P and P’s identity
•    3P can only sue P
Partially Disclosed Principal
•    3P knows A is working for P but does not know P’s identity
•    3P can sue P or A

Undisclosed Principal
•  3P does not know A is working for P or P’s identity
•  3P can only sue A
UNDERLYING CAUSE OF ACTION: TORT CLAIM

Vicarious Liability
•
P liable for employee’s torts committed within scope of employment
•
Generally, not liable for independent contractor’s torts

Scope of Employment
(1) Conduct occurred within general limitations of employment
(2) A motivated to act for P’s benefit
(3) Act within A’s assigned duties

Intentional Torts
•
P may be liable for employee’s intentional torts

Work-Related Travel
•
Commuting for business is within scope of employment
Direct Liability
•
P directly liable to 3P if
(i) Consented or approved of A’s tortious conduct toward 3P
(ii) Negligently hired or managed A, or
(iii) Assigned a non-delegable duty

blocks.text:
Agent’s Tort Liability
•
A’s negligence torts: A directly liable, P vicariously liable
•
P can seek indemnification from A
FIDUCIARY DUTIES

Agent’s Duties Owed to Principals
(i)  Duty of loyalty
(ii)  Duty to obey K
(iii)  Duty of rx care
(iv)  Duty to preserve trade secrets
(v)  Duty to provide info
(vi)  Duty to act within scope of authority
(vii) Indemnity for A’s own torts

Duty of Loyalty
•
A cannot
•
Usurp business opportunity
•
Collect secret profits
•
Compete against P
 Agent’s Rights

(i) Compensation
(ii) Reimbursement
(iii) Indemnity
(iv) P’s good faith and cooperation
(v) P preventing harm to A

blocks.text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
•
SMJ required for federal courts to hear case
•
Exists if
•
Federal question
•
Diversity of citizenship, or
•
Supplemental jx
•
Not waivable

California – Subject Matter Jurisdiction
•
SMJ over all civil cases unless within exclusive jx of another court
Classification of Cases

Unlimited
•
AIC > $25,000
Limited
•
AIC ≤ $25,000
Small Claims
•
Natural persons and sole proprietors: AIC ≤ $10,000
•
Business entities: AIC ≤ $5,000
Federal Question

•
Civil actions arising under Constitution, laws, or treaties of U.S.
•
Must appear in P’s COA and on face of well-pleaded complaint

Diversity Jurisdiction
(1) Complete diversity between parties, and
(2) AIC > $75,000

Complete Diversity
•
All Ps citizens of different state than all Ds
•
Determined at time suit is filed

Natural Persons
•
Citizenship is place of domicile
(1) Reside in state or U.S. territory, and
(2) Intent to remain

pages.normalized_text:
CIVIL PROCEDURE

SUBJECT MATTER JURISDICTION

  ✷  Subject Matter Jurisdiction
       •  SMJ required for federal courts to hear case
       •   Exists if
           •   Federal question
           •   Diversity of citizenship, or
           •   Supplemental jx
       •  Not waivable

                California – Subject Matter Jurisdiction
              •  SMJ over all civil cases unless within exclusive jx of another court

                         Classification of Cases

                             Unlimited
                            •  AIC > $25,000

                              Limited
                            •  AIC ≤ $25,000

                             Small Claims
                            •   Natural persons and sole proprietors: AIC ≤ $10,000
                            •   Business entities: AIC ≤ $5,000

  ✷  Federal Question
       •   Civil actions arising under Constitution, laws, or treaties of U.S.
       •  Must appear in P’s COA and on face of well-pleaded complaint

  ✷  Diversity Jurisdiction
        (1) Complete diversity between parties, and
        (2) AIC > $75,000

      ✷  Complete Diversity
              •   All Ps citizens of different state than all Ds
              •  Determined at time suit is filed

          ✷  Natural Persons
                     •   Citizenship is place of domicile
                             (1) Reside in state or U.S. territory, and
                             (2) Intent to remain

                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
Permanent Resident Aliens
•
Citizenship is place of domicile
•
Reside in state or U.S. territory, and
•
Intent to remain

Non-Permanent Resident Aliens
•
At least 1 foreign citizen vs. at least 1 state citizen

Corporations
•
Citizen of
(1) Every state in which incorporated, and
(2) State where maintains PPB

Foreign Corporations
•
Citizen of
(1) Every country in which incorporated, and
(2) State where maintains PPB if in U.S.
Unincorporated Associations
•
Citizens of all states of which its partners, members, or owners are
citizens

Amount in Controversy
•
AIC > $75,000
•
Determined at time suit is filed
Aggregation of Claims
Single Plaintiff v. Single Defendant
•
P can aggregate all claims against D to satisfy AIC

Single Plaintiff v. Multiple Defendants
•
P can aggregate claims only if Ds are joint tortfeasors

Multiple Plaintiffs v. Single Defendant
•
Ps may aggregate claims only if “enforcing a single title or right of
common or undivided interest”

Supplemental Jurisdiction
•
Federal courts can hear additional claims as long as same case or controversy as original
claim

✷  Permanent Resident Aliens
                         •   Citizenship is place of domicile
                             •   Reside in state or U.S. territory, and
                             •   Intent to remain

            ✷  Non-Permanent Resident Aliens
                         •  At least 1 foreign citizen vs. at least 1 state citizen

        ✷  Corporations
                  •   Citizen of
                         (1) Every state in which incorporated, and
                         (2) State where maintains PPB

            ✷  Foreign Corporations
                         •   Citizen of
                                  (1) Every country in which incorporated, and
                                  (2) State where maintains PPB if in U.S.

            ✷  Unincorporated Associations
                         •   Citizens of all states of which its partners, members, or owners are
                                  citizens

    ✷  Amount in Controversy
           •  AIC > $75,000
           •  Determined at time suit is filed

        ✷  Aggregation of Claims

            ✷  Single Plaintiff v. Single Defendant
                         •  P can aggregate all claims against D to satisfy AIC

            ✷  Single Plaintiff v. Multiple Defendants
                         •  P can aggregate claims only if Ds are joint tortfeasors

            ✷  Multiple Plaintiffs v. Single Defendant
                         •   Ps may aggregate claims only if “enforcing a single title or right of
                      common or undivided interest”

✷  Supplemental Jurisdiction
    •   Federal courts can hear additional claims as long as same case or controversy as original
       claim

blocks.text:
Federal Question
•
Supp jx over additional parties or claims if same case or controversy
Diversity
•
Always supp jx if
•
Same case or controversy, and
•
Does not destroy complete diversity
Discretion to Decline Jurisdiction
•
Federal courts may decline to exercise supp jx
REMOVAL AND REMAND
Removal – State Court to Federal Court

•
D may remove case if
•
 P’s claims could have been filed in federal court, and
•
Ds unanimously consent
Diversity Jurisdiction
•
If jx based on diversity, removal only proper if no D is citizen of state in which
action pending
Remand – Federal Court to State Court

Plaintiff’s Motion to Remand
•
P may move to remand if removal improper

Timing
•
Motion must be made within 30 days after filing notice of removal
PERSONAL JURISDICTION

Personal Jurisdiction
•
Court’s authority over particular D or property

Voluntary Presence
•
PJ over individual D served while voluntarily present in state
Exception
•
Immunity to Ds in state exclusively for other judicial proceedings

blocks.text:
Domicile
•
PJ over Ds domiciled in state

Corporations
•
Citizen of
•
Every state in which incorporated, and
•
State where maintains PPB
Consent
•
D may consent to PJ

Waiver
•
PJ waived if D voluntarily appears to contest case without objecting to jx

Long-Arm Statutes
•
Grant courts PJ over out-of-state Ds who engage in some activity in state
•
Unlimited or limited

Constitutional Requirements
•
Out-of-state D must
•
Have minimum contacts with forum state, and
•
Maintenance of action does not offend traditional notions of fair play and
substantial justice
Minimum Contacts
General Jurisdiction

•
Contacts with forum state so systematic, continuous, and substantial
that D at home in that state
Individuals
•
Must be domiciled in forum state

Corporations
•
Where incorporated or headquartered
Specific Jurisdiction
(1) D has purposely availed himself to forum state, and
(2) COA arises out of D’s contact in that state

blocks.text:
Purposeful Availment
•
D purposely avails himself of state’s benefits and protections

•
D must rxly anticipate he could be haled into court

Fair Play and Substantial Justice
•
Must be fair and rx
•
Factors

(i)
Burden on D
(ii) Interest of forum state
(iii) P’s interest in obtaining relief
(iv) Interest of judicial system
(v) Shared policy interests of states
Notice

•
D must be given rx notice for opportunity to be heard
VENUE

Venue
•
Venue is proper
(i) In judicial district where any D resides, if all Ds reside in same state
(ii) In judicial district in which substantial part of events occurred, or
(iii) Where any D is subject to PJ

blocks.text:
California – Venue
Local Actions
•
Venue proper in county where property is located

Individual
•
Venue proper
(i) In county where any D resides at commencement of action, or
(ii) Any county, if no Ds reside in CA
Personal Injury or Wrongful Death
•
Venue proper
(i) Where personal injury occurred
(ii) In county where any D resides at commencement of action, or
(iii) In any county, if no Ds reside in CA

Contract Claims
•
Venue proper
(i) Where K entered into
(ii) Where K to be performed
(iii) In county where any D resides at commencement of action, or
(iv) In any county, if no Ds reside in CA
Corporations
•
Venue proper

(i) Where K made or to be performed
(ii) Where liability arose or breach occurred, or
(iii) PPB

Whether New Venue is Proper
Venue analysis applies.  See above.

Interest of Justice
Public Factors
(i) Administrative difficulties
(ii) Interest in having cases decided locally
(iii) Familiarity with forum law
(iv) Avoidance of conflict of laws

Private Factors
(i) Accessibility to evidence
(ii) Ability to compel attendance of witnesses
(iii) Cost of attendance for witnesses
Venue Transfer – Original Venue Improper
•
May transfer case to any district or division where it could have originally been
brought
•
Timely objection required

Venue analysis applies.  See above.

Interest of Justice
See above.
Public Factors
See above.

Forum Selection Clause
•
Generally honored by courts unless against interests of justice

Venue Transfer – Choice of Law
Diversity Cases
•
Transferee courts apply substantive law that transferring court would have
applied
Federal Question Cases
•
Transferee courts use law they would have applied if case originally filed
there

blocks.text:
California – Venue Transfer
•
D may move to transfer if
(i) Original venue is improper, or
(ii) D shows actual prejudice
ERIE DOCTRINE

Erie Doctrine
•
Case based on diversity jx
•
Substantive issues: State law
•
Procedural issues: Federal law

Nature of Issue Unclear
•
Tests
•
Whether rule is outcome determinative
•
Weigh interests of state and federal judicial authorities
•
Whether application of federal law will encourage forum shopping
California – Conflicts of Law Rule

Torts
•
Courts determine which state’s law should be applied by evaluating impairment to
each state’s interest

Contracts
•
Generally, choice of law provision applies
•
If no choice of law provision, courts determine which state’s law should be applied
by evaluating impairment to each state’s interest
PLEADINGS

Amendments as a Matter of Course
•
Party may amend
(i) Once within 21 days of service, or
(ii) Within 21 days after service of responsive pleading
•
Otherwise, leave of court required
California – Amendments
•
Party may amend once without leave of court at any time before responsive
pleading is filed

Additional Claim
•
Amendment to pleading relates back if
(i) Law the provides applicable SOL allows relation back, or
(ii) Amendment asserts claim or defense that arose out of conduct set out in

original pleading
Change of Party or Party Name
•
Amendment relates back if
•
Amendment asserts claim or defense that arose out of conduct set out in
original pleading
•
New party received notice within 90 days of filing, and
•
Party knew that the action would have been brought, but for a mistake re
identity
California – Relation Back
Claims
•
Party may amend even if SOL has run out if additional claim
concerns
(1) Same general facts
(2) Same injury, and
(3) Same offending instrumentality
Parties
•
Party may amend even if SOL has run out if he
(1) Named Does in original complaint
(2) Includes charging allegations
(3) Was truly ignorant of D’s identity
(4) Acts with rx diligence, and
(5) Amends within 3 years
MOTIONS

Motion for Summary Judgment
•
Granted if no genuine dispute of material fact
DISCOVERY

blocks.text:
•
Non-privileged matter that is
•
Relevant to any party’s claim or defense, and
•
Proportional to needs of case
Mandatory Disclosures

Initial Disclosures
•
Must be provided to opposing party within 14 days after conference
•
Include
(i) Names and contact info of individuals likely to have discoverable info
(ii) Copies or descriptions of relevant docs, ESI, property
(iii) Calculation of damages
(iv) Insurance agreements

Expert Testimony Disclosures
•
Must disclose identity of any expert witnesses at least 90 days before trial

Pretrial Disclosures
•
Must be disclosed by each party 30 days before trial
•
Include
(i) Witnesses’ names and contact info
(ii) Designation of witnesses expected for deposition
(iii) Docs or exhibits expected for trial
Discovery Devices

Depositions
•
Party may conduct up to 10 depos limited to 1 day and not to exceed 7 hours
Physical or Mental Examinations
•
May order party to submit to examination if mental or physical condition in
controversy
Motion to Compel Disclosure or Discovery
•
Party may move for order compelling disclosure or discovery
•
If party fails to comply, court may impose sanctions

blocks.text:
Qualified Immunity
•
Docs prepared in anticipation of litigation not discoverable unless
•
Substantial need, and
•
Undue hardship

Absolute Immunity
•
Lawyer’s mental impressions, research, opinions, etc. not discoverable unless
immunity waived

Compulsory Joinder
•
Party must be joined if
(i) Court cannot grant complete relief to existing parties, or
(ii) Party claims absence may impair ability to protect interest

Joinder Not Feasible
•
Court must decide if action should proceed or be dismissed

Factors
(i) Extent to which judgment in party’s absence would prejudice parties
(ii) Extent to which prejudice could be reduced
(iii) Whether adequate judgment could be rendered
(iv) Whether P will have adequate remedy
Permissive Joinder
•
Ps or Ds can be joined if
(1) They assert any right jointly, severally, or arising out of same transaction or

occurrence, and
(2) Any question of law or fact common to all parties

pages.normalized_text:
PRIVILEGES

        Privileges

           Work Product

                       Qualified Immunity
                     •  Docs prepared in anticipation of litigation not discoverable unless
                         •   Substantial need, and
                         •  Undue hardship

                      Absolute Immunity
                     •  Lawyer’s mental impressions, research, opinions, etc. not discoverable unless
                        immunity waived

JOINDER

        Joinder of Parties

             Compulsory Joinder
              •   Party must be joined if
                          (i)  Court cannot grant complete relief to existing parties, or
                           (ii) Party claims absence may impair ability to protect interest

                       Joinder Not Feasible
                     •   Court must decide if action should proceed or be dismissed

                               Factors
                                          (i)  Extent to which judgment in party’s absence would prejudice parties
                                           (ii) Extent to which prejudice could be reduced
                                            (iii) Whether adequate judgment could be rendered
                                    (iv) Whether P will have adequate remedy

               Permissive Joinder
              •   Ps or Ds can be joined if
                     (1) They assert any right jointly, severally, or arising out of same transaction or
                       occurrence, and
                     (2) Any question of law or fact common to all parties

                     Supplemental Jurisdiction
                     •   Ps: Applicable
                     •   Ds: Not applicable

blocks.text:
Intervention of Right
•
3P intervention must be permitted if
(i) Unconditional right to intervene by federal statute, or
(ii) Claims interest that is subject matter of lawsuit, the disposition of which without
3P would impair ability to protect interest

Permissive Intervention
•
3P intervention may be permitted if
(i) Conditional right to intervene by federal statute, or
(ii) Claim shares common question of law or fact with main action

Interpleader
• Allows 3P stakeholder to compel parties claiming competing property interests to litigate
claim

Rule 22 Interpleader
•
Requires SMJ through federal question or diversity jx

Statutory Interpleader
•
SMJ granted if at least 1 claimant diverse from another and AIC > $500
• Allows D to bring in 3PD who may be liable to D for all or part of P’s claim

Jurisdiction
•
Requires SMJ over claims asserted against 3PD and claims brought by 3PD against
other parties

Joinder of Claims
•
Party may join as many claims it has against opposing party
•
Requires SMJ and proper venue

Diversity Jurisdiction
•
P may aggregate all claims to meet AIC

Federal Question Jurisdiction
•
State claims may be joined with federal claim if
(i) Diversity jx, or
(ii) Same transaction or occurrence

       •  3P intervention may be permitted if
                (i)  Conditional right to intervene by federal statute, or
                (ii) Claim shares common question of law or fact with main action


Interpleader
•  Allows 3P stakeholder to compel parties claiming competing property interests to litigate
   claim

       Rule 22 Interpleader
       •   Requires SMJ through federal question or diversity jx

        Statutory Interpleader
       •  SMJ granted if at least 1 claimant diverse from another and AIC > $500

Impleader
•  Allows D to bring in 3PD who may be liable to D for all or part of P’s claim


        Jurisdiction
       •   Requires SMJ over claims asserted against 3PD and claims brought by 3PD against
            other parties

Joinder of Claims
•   Party may join as many claims it has against opposing party
•   Requires SMJ and proper venue

        Diversity Jurisdiction
       •  P may aggregate all claims to meet AIC

        Federal Question Jurisdiction
       •   State claims may be joined with federal claim if
                (i)  Diversity jx, or
                (ii) Same transaction or occurrence





                                   12                                          Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                              sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
Counterclaims
•
Claims D brings against P
•
Requires SMJ

Compulsory
•
Party must state claims that
(1) Arise out of same transaction or occurrence as facts in opposing party’s claim,

and
(2) Do not require adding another party over whom court cannot acquire jx
Permissive
•
Party may state claims that are not compulsory
•
Requires SMJ

Crossclaims
•
May state claim by one party against co-party if
(i) Same transaction or occurrence, or
(ii) Relates to property that is subject of original action
CLASS ACTIONS

Class Actions
Prerequisites
(1) Numerosity
(2) Commonality
(3) Typicality, and
(4) Adequacy
Class Action Fairness Act (2005)
(1) Any member diverse from any D
(2) Claims > $5 million, and
(3) Members  100
TRIAL

Right to a Jury Trial
•
Right to jury trial where AIC > $20
Judgment as a Matter of Law
•
Granted when party fully heard on issue and no rx jury would find for party on that issue
•
Can move any time before case submitted to jury

blocks.text:
Renewed Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law
•
If JMOL not granted, may be renewed no later than 28 days after entry of judgment

Final Judgement Rule
•
Party may only appeal final judgments
•
Ends entire litigation on merits, leaving court nothing to do but execute judgment

Claim Preclusion
(1) Same parties
(2) Same COA, and
(3) Valid, final judgment on the merits

Same Parties
•
Identical parties or persons in privity with original parties

Same Cause of Action
•
Same with respect to all or part of claim

Valid, Final Judgement on the Merits
Valid
•
Rendered by court with SMJ, PJ, and proper venue
•
D given proper notice and hearing

Final Judgement on the Merits
•
Based on factual and legal disputes
Issue Preclusion
(1) Same issue
(2) Actually litigated and final judgment
(3) Essential to judgment, and
(4) Party against whom preclusion sought was party in previous lawsuit
Same Issue
•
Identical to issue in prior claim

Actually Litigated & Determined by Final Judgment
•
Presented to judge or jury

blocks.text:
General Community Property Principles
•
Marital economic community begins upon marriage and ends upon divorce, death, or
permanent physical separation
•
CP: Acquired during marriage
•
SP:
•
Earned before marriage, during separation, or following divorce
•
Inheritances, gifts, devises, bequests acquired during marriage
•
QCP: Acquired by spouse/couple while domiciled in non-CP state that would have been CP if
acquired while domiciled in CA

(1) Consent of both parties
(2) Parties are at least 18 years old, and
(3) Parties have capacity

Permanent Separation
(1) One or both spouses express intent to end marriage, and
(2) Actions consistent with intent

•
Entitled to same rights and benefits as married couples

•
CP law not applied
•
K law applies

•
No legal marriage
•
Good faith belief marriage is valid

Rights
•
Same rights as spouse of valid marriage

pages.normalized_text:
COMMUNITY PROPERTY

GENERAL COMMUNITY PROPERTY PRINCIPLES

  ✷  General Community Property Principles
       •   Marital economic community begins upon marriage and ends upon divorce, death, or
          permanent physical separation
       •  CP: Acquired during marriage
       •   SP:
           •  Earned before marriage, during separation, or following divorce
           •   Inheritances, gifts, devises, bequests acquired during marriage
       •  QCP: Acquired by spouse/couple while domiciled in non-CP state that would have been CP if
           acquired while domiciled in CA

MARRIED AND UNMARRIED COUPLES

  ✷ Married Couples
        (1) Consent of both parties
        (2) Parties are at least 18 years old, and
        (3) Parties have capacity

      ✷ End of the Marital Community
              •   Divorce, death, or permanent separation

          ✷  Permanent Separation
                         (1) One or both spouses express intent to end marriage, and
                         (2) Actions consistent with intent

      Domestic Partnerships
       •   Entitled to same rights and benefits as married couples

  ✷ Unmarried Cohabitants
       •  CP law not applied
       •  K law applies

  ✷ Putative Spouses
       •  No legal marriage
       •  Good faith belief marriage is valid

      ✷  Rights
              •  Same rights as spouse of valid marriage

                                          1                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
Quasi-Marital Property
•
Would have been CP or QCP had marriage been valid
•
Treated same as CP

•
Same rights as if surviving lawful spouse

Lawful and Putative Spouse
•
Estate distributed equally between claimants

(1) In writing
(2) Signed by both parties
(3) Executed voluntarily, and
(4) Not unconscionable

(i) Represented by independent counsel, or
(ii)
Advised to consult counsel at least 7 days before execution and representation
expressly waived

(1) Not adequately informed re other party’s finances, and
(2) No waiver of disclosure of finances

Quasi-Community Property
•
Acquired by spouse/couple while domiciled in non-CP state that would have been CP
if acquired while domiciled in CA
•
Characterized as QCP upon divorce or death of spouse holding title

•
Earned before marriage, during separation, or following divorce
•
Inheritances, gifts, devises, bequests acquired during marriage
•
Income through SP asset

pages.normalized_text:
✷  Quasi-Marital Property
              •  Would have been CP or QCP had marriage been valid
              •   Treated same as CP

      ✷  Separate Property
              •  Same rights as if surviving lawful spouse

      ✷  Lawful and Putative Spouse
              •   Estate distributed equally between claimants

PREMARITAL AGREEMENTS

  ✷  Premarital Agreements
        (1) In writing
        (2) Signed by both parties
        (3) Executed voluntarily, and
        (4) Not unconscionable

      ✷  Voluntary
                     (i)  Represented by independent counsel, or
                     (ii)  Advised to consult counsel at least 7 days before execution and representation
                    expressly waived

      ✷  Unconscionable
                 (1) Not adequately informed re other party’s finances, and
                 (2) No waiver of disclosure of finances

PRESUMPTIONS

       General Community Property Presumption
       •  CP: Property acquired during marriage

             Quasi-Community Property
              •   Acquired by spouse/couple while domiciled in non-CP state that would have been CP
                           if acquired while domiciled in CA
              •   Characterized as QCP upon divorce or death of spouse holding title

        Separate Property Presumption
       •  Earned before marriage, during separation, or following divorce
       •   Inheritances, gifts, devises, bequests acquired during marriage
       •  Income through SP asset

blocks.text:
Anti-Lucas (Post-1984)
•
Property held jointly by spouses presumed to be CP at divorce
•
Rebuttable by express writing
•
SP contributions: Not gifts
•
Contributing spouse entitled to reimbursement

Lucas (Pre-1984)
•
Property held jointly by spouses presumed to be CP at divorce
•
Rebuttable by demonstration that property not intended to be CP
•
SP contributions: Gifts
•
Contributing spouse not entitled to reimbursement

Tracing
Tip: Analyze every change in form or identity of property

(1) In writing
(2) Signed by party who would be adversely affected, and
(3) Express declaration that character of property will be changed

Exception – Gifts
(1) Gift between spouses
(2) Limited to clothing, jewelry, or other tangible and personal items
(3) Used primarily by receiving spouse, and
(4) Not substantial in value relative – look to circumstances of marriage

•
When source of asset was CP, character not changed even if title taken in one spouse’s name
Equal Rights and Management Over Community Property

•
Spouses have equal rights in management of CP

Gifts to Third Parties
•
Consent required for unilateral gift or sale of CP for less than FMV
•
If no consent, non-consenting spouse can ratify gift or revoke and sue to recover

blocks.text:
Sale or Lease of Community Real Property
•
Sale or lease of CP real property for 1+ year requires written agreement signed by
both spouses

Sale of Community Business Assets
•
Manager spouse of CP business has sole management rights unless intends to sell
substantial part or all

•
Duty to fully disclose material facts about community assets and debts
•
Duty to never seek unfair advantage over the other

•
Failure to obtain consent when selling or leasing property or making gifts
SEPARATE PROPERTY AND COMMUNITY PROPERTY BUSINESSES
Separate Property Businesses (Community Labor into an SP Business)

Pereira Method
•
Appreciation accredited to spouse’s management

SP = SP business’s value at time of marriage + (SP business’s value at time
of marriage × rate of return × years married)
CP = fair market value of the SP business upon divorce – SP

Van Camp Method
•
Appreciation accredited to market and industry
CP = (reasonable salary – yearly family expenses) x years married
SP = fair market value of the business upon divorce – CP
When Community Property Business Increases in Value After Separation

CP = the CP business’s value at time of separation + (value of CP at time
of separation x rate of return x years separated)
SP = fair market value of business upon divorce – CP

                  both spouses

      ✷  Sale of Community Business Assets

                     substantial part or all

  ✷  Fiduciary Duties
       •  Duty to fully disclose material facts about community assets and debts
       •  Duty to never seek unfair advantage over the other

          Breach
       •   Failure to obtain consent when selling or leasing property or making gifts

SPECIFIC TYPES OF PROPERTY

     SEPARATE PROPERTY AND COMMUNITY PROPERTY BUSINESSES

      ✷  Separate Property Businesses (Community Labor into an SP Business)

          ✷  Reverse Pereira Method

                        of separation x rate of return x years separated)

blocks.text:
SP = reasonable salary during separation – family expenses during
separation
CP = fair market value of business upon divorce – SP

Community Property to Improve Separate Property
•
CP contribution to SP
•
Community reimbursed or appreciation in property value, whichever is
greater

Separate Property to Improve Other Spouse’s Separate Property
•
SP contribution to SP
•
Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution unless transmutation or written
waiver

Separate Property to Improve Community Property
•
SP contribution to CP
•
Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution if successfully traced

Credit and Loans
•
Community responsible for loans taken out during marriage

Commingled Bank Accounts
•
Presumed CP unless tracing demonstrates otherwise

Direct Tracing Method
(1) Adequate SP funds available when asset acquired, and
(2) Intent to acquire asset using SP

Indirect Tracing Method
•
Presumed CP funds used first for community expenses

pages.normalized_text:
✷  Reverse Van Camp Method

                 SP = reasonable salary during separation – family expenses during
                      separation

               CP = fair market value of business upon divorce – SP

IMPROVEMENTS TO REAL PROPERTY

      ✷  Improvements

          ✷  Community Property to Improve Separate Property
                     •  CP contribution to SP
                     •  Community reimbursed or appreciation in property value, whichever is
                             greater

          ✷  Separate Property to Improve Other Spouse’s Separate Property
                     •  SP contribution to SP
                     •  Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution unless transmutation or written
                         waiver

          ✷  Separate Property to Improve Community Property
                     •  SP contribution to CP
                     •  Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution if successfully traced

CREDIT AND LOANS

        Credit and Loans
       •  Community responsible for loans taken out during marriage

COMMINGLED BANK ACCOUNTS

      Commingled Bank Accounts
       •  Presumed CP unless tracing demonstrates otherwise

                Direct Tracing Method
                 (1) Adequate SP funds available when asset acquired, and
                 (2) Intent to acquire asset using SP

                 Indirect Tracing Method
              •  Presumed CP funds used first for community expenses

                     •  Community reimbursed or appreciation in property value, whichever is
                             greater

          ✷  Separate Property to Improve Other Spouse’s Separate Property
                     •  SP contribution to SP
                     •  Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution unless transmutation or written
                         waiver

          ✷  Separate Property to Improve Community Property
                     •  SP contribution to CP
                     •  Spouse reimbursed for SP contribution if successfully traced

CREDIT AND LOANS

        Credit and Loans
       •  Community responsible for loans taken out during marriage

COMMINGLED BANK ACCOUNTS

      Commingled Bank Accounts
       •  Presumed CP unless tracing demonstrates otherwise

                Direct Tracing Method
                 (1) Adequate SP funds available when asset acquired, and
                 (2) Intent to acquire asset using SP

                 Indirect Tracing Method
              •  Presumed CP funds used first for community expenses





                                          5                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
Educational Degrees
•
Community entitled to reimbursement if
(1) CP or QCP funds used to pay for education
(2) Education substantially enhances spouse’s earning capacity, and
(3) No written agreement to the contrary

Defenses
•
Community already substantially benefited
•
CP funds also used for other spouse’s education
•
Education reduced need for spousal support

Cause of Action Against Third Party
•
COA arose during marriage: Recovery is CP
•
Upon divorce: Recovery awarded to injured spouses unless (i) commingled funds or (ii) share
awarded to non-injured spouse in equity
•
COA arose before or after marriage: Recovery is SP

Cause of Action Against Spouse
•
Personal injury recovery is injured spouse’s SP

Past Efforts (Hug Formula)
•
Granted as payment for past services

CP = (years between date hired and date separated) y [(years between date
hired and date options become exercisable) x amount of shares]

Retention Incentive (Nelson Formula)
•
Granted to encourage continued employment

CP = (years between date options were granted and date of separation) y
[(years between date options were granted and date options become
exercisable) x amount of shares]

pages.normalized_text:
EDUCATIONAL DEGREES

  ✷  Educational Degrees
       •  Community entitled to reimbursement if
             (1) CP or QCP funds used to pay for education
             (2) Education substantially enhances spouse’s earning capacity, and
             (3) No written agreement to the contrary

      ✷  Defenses
              •  Community already substantially benefited
              •  CP funds also used for other spouse’s education
              •   Education reduced need for spousal support

PERSONAL INJURY RECOVERY

  ✷  Cause of Action Against Third Party
       •  COA arose during marriage: Recovery is CP
       •  Upon divorce: Recovery awarded to injured spouses unless (i) commingled funds or (ii) share
          awarded to non-injured spouse in equity
       •  COA arose before or after marriage: Recovery is SP

  ✷  Cause of Action Against Spouse
       •   Personal injury recovery is injured spouse’s SP

STOCK OPTIONS

  ✷  Stock Options

      ✷  Past Efforts (Hug Formula)
              •   Granted as payment for past services

               CP = (years between date hired and date separated) y [(years between date
                      hired and date options become exercisable) x amount of shares]

                 SP = amount of shares – CP

      ✷  Retention Incentive (Nelson Formula)
              •   Granted to encourage continued employment

               CP = (years between date options were granted and date of separation) y
                       [(years between date options were granted and date options become
                       exercisable) x amount of shares]

                 SP = amount of shares – CP

blocks.text:
Whole Life
•
Commingled policy allocated based on how many CP and SP funds used to pay for premiums

Term Life
•
Characterized based on whether CP or SP funds used to pay final premium

Goodwill
•
If accumulated during marriage, goodwill is CP
•
For value, look to FMV or business’s excess earnings as result of goodwill

Division of Property at Divorce
•
Community estate divided equally between spouses
•
Exceptions
•
Deliberate misappropriation
•
Liabilities > assets
•
Education debts
•
Personal injury recovery
•
Domestic violence damages
•
Debt incurred before marriage or after separation
•
Parent with custody receives family residence

Division of Property at Death – Probate and Intestacy
See Wills Template.

Community Property Liability
•
Marital economic community subject to debts incurred before or during marriage

Separate Property Liability
•
Creditors may access SP of debtor spouse but not that of non-debtor spouse

Divorce
•
Debtor spouse responsible for debts incurred before marriage and those incurred during
marriage that did not benefit community

pages.normalized_text:
LIFE INSURANCE

      Whole Life
       •  Commingled policy allocated based on how many CP and SP funds used to pay for premiums

      Term Life
       •   Characterized based on whether CP or SP funds used to pay final premium

GOODWILL

  ✷  Goodwill
       •    If accumulated during marriage, goodwill is CP
       •   For value, look to FMV or business’s excess earnings as result of goodwill

DISTRIBUTION

  ✷  Division of Property at Divorce
       •  Community estate divided equally between spouses
       •   Exceptions
           •   Deliberate misappropriation
           •   Liabilities > assets
           •   Education debts
           •   Personal injury recovery
           •  Domestic violence damages
           •  Debt incurred before marriage or after separation
           •   Parent with custody receives family residence

        Division of Property at Death – Probate and Intestacy
       See Wills Template.

DEBTS

  ✷  Community Property Liability
       •   Marital economic community subject to debts incurred before or during marriage

  ✷  Separate Property Liability
       •   Creditors may access SP of debtor spouse but not that of non-debtor spouse

  ✷  Divorce
       •   Debtor spouse responsible for debts incurred before marriage and those incurred during
           marriage that did not benefit community

SPECIALTextRULES

blocks.text:
Necessaries of Life
•
Non-debtor spouse’s CP and SP liable for debts incurred by debtor spouse for necessaries of
life

Separation
•
Non-debtor spouse’s CP and SP still liable for debts incurred by debtor spouse for
necessaries of life

Child and Spousal Support
•
Child or spousal support obligation not arising from marriage treated as debt incurred before
marriage

Tort Obligations
•
SP and CP liable for torts committed by either spouse during marriage

pages.normalized_text:

  ✷  Necessaries of Life
       •   Non-debtor spouse’s CP and SP liable for debts incurred by debtor spouse for necessaries of
              life

      ✷  Separation

                   necessaries of life

      ✷  Divorce
              •   Liability severed

       •   Child or spousal support obligation not arising from marriage treated as debt incurred before
          marriage

blocks.text:
STATE ACTION
State Action
•
Required to invoke constitutional protections
•
Involves action by
•
Government or government official, or
•
Private party entangled with government

Eleventh Amendment – Sovereign Immunity
Private Parties
•
Private party cannot sue state or its agency
•
Exceptions
•
State waives sovereign immunity
•
Lawsuit involves federal laws under Section 5 of 14A
•
Lawsuit seeks declaratory or injunctive relief against named state
official
•
Lawsuit seeks money damages from named state official
Congressional Abrogation
•
Congress may regulate state laws violating due process, equal
protection, and privileges and immunities
•
Congress must expressly state abrogation
STANDING
Standing
(1) Injury-in-fact
(2) Causation, and
(3) Redressability
Generalized Grievances
•
Generalized grievances lack standing

Third Party Standing
•
Generally, no standing to assert 3P rights
•
Exceptions
•
3P unable to assert own rights
•
Special relationship between P and 3P

pages.normalized_text:
CONSTITUTIONAL LAW

JUSTICIABILITY

     STATE ACTION

      ✷  State Action
              •   Required to invoke constitutional protections
              •   Involves action by
                  •  Government or government official, or
                  •   Private party entangled with government

          ✷  Eleventh Amendment – Sovereign Immunity

              ✷  Private Parties
                            •   Private party cannot sue state or its agency
                            •   Exceptions
                                •   State waives sovereign immunity
                                •   Lawsuit involves federal laws under Section 5 of 14A
                                •   Lawsuit seeks declaratory or injunctive relief against named state
                                             official
                                •   Lawsuit seeks money damages from named state official

              ✷  Congressional Abrogation
                            •   Congress may regulate state laws violating due process, equal
                                      protection, and privileges and immunities
                            •   Congress must expressly state abrogation

     STANDING

      ✷  Standing
                 (1) Injury-in-fact
                 (2) Causation, and
                 (3) Redressability

          ✷  Generalized Grievances
                     •   Generalized grievances lack standing

      ✷  Third Party Standing
              •   Generally, no standing to assert 3P rights
              •   Exceptions
                  •  3P unable to assert own rights
                  •   Special relationship between P and 3P

blocks.text:
•
Harm to P adversely affects relationship with 3P
Organizational Standing
•
Standing if harm is to organization itself
Organizations on Behalf of its Members
(1) Members would have standing to sue individually
(2) Harm related to organization’s purpose, and
(3) Neither claim nor relief sought necessitates individual members’

participation
RIPENESS
Ripeness
•
Harm or threat of imminent harm
MOOTNESS
Mootness
•
Live case and controversy at all stages of review

Exceptions
Capable of Repetition Yet Evading Review
•
Harm exists in short periods and ceases prior to end of litigation
•
May be repeated

Voluntary Cessation
•
D voluntarily ceases harm to P but can resume at any time
Class Actions
•
Not dismissed where at least one class member has ongoing injury
SEPARATION OF POWERS
LEGISLATIVE BRANCH
NECESSARY AND PROPER CLAUSE
Necessary and Proper Clause
•
Congress has power to enact laws necessary and proper to execute powers of
federal government

blocks.text:
COMMERCE CLAUSE
Commerce Clause – Interstate Commerce
•
Congress has power to regulate channels, instrumentalities, and economic
activities that substantially affect interstate commerce

Economic or Commercial Activities
•
Congress may regulate if rational basis that activities, in aggregate,
substantially affect interstate commerce
Non-Economic or Non-Commercial Activities
•
Congress may regulate if can demonstrate substantial economic
effect on interstate commerce

Substantial Economic Effect – Aggregation
•
Congress may regulate any interstate or intrastate activity that has a
substantial economic effect on interstate commerce
TENTH AMENDMENT AND COMMANDEERING
Tenth Amendment
•
All powers neither granted to federal government nor prohibited to the states
are reserved to the states
Commandeering
•
Congress may not commandeer state legislatures to enact legislation or
execute a federal regulatory program
TAXING AND SPENDING
Taxing and Spending Power
•
Congress may tax and spend for the general welfare
Taxes
(1) Tax’s primary purpose is to raise revenue, and
(2) Rx relationship between tax and regulation
Regulatory Spending
•
For any public purpose
Conditional Grants
•
Encourage, not compel, state legislation

blocks.text:
•
Condition
(1) Unambiguously stated
(2) Bear relationship to spending purpose, and
(3) Not unduly coercive
DELEGATION OF POWER
Delegation
•
Congress may delegate legislative powers to executive officers and
administrative agencies
•
Must provide intelligible standards to establish scope
EXECUTIVE BRANCH
Executive Powers
•
Reserved to the President
Domestic Affairs
•
Appoint and remove officers
•
Pardon offenses against U.S.
•
Execute federal law

Foreign Affairs
•
Act on behalf of U.S. in foreign relations
•
Deploy as commander-in-chief of armed forces
•
Negotiate and enter into executive agreements
APPOINTMENT AND REMOVAL
Appointment Powers
President
•
Appoint ambassadors, top-level federal officers, federal judges
•
With advice and consent of Senate
Congress
•
Can vest power to appoint inferior officers in other branches of
government
Removal Powers
President
•
Can remove top-level executive officers without cause

•
Congress can require showing of good cause
Congress
•
Can only remove through impeachment power
PARDON POWER
Pardon Power
•
Power to grant pardons
TREATIES AND EXECUTIVE AGREEMENTS
Treaties
•
President has authority to make treaties
•
Must be ratified by 2/3 Senate
•
Treaties > Executive agreements

Conflicts
•
Treaties > Conflicting state laws
•
Treaty vs. Congressional acts: Whichever is most recent
•
Constitution > Treaties
Executive Agreements
•
Between President and heads of foreign countries
Conflicts
•
Executive agreements > Conflicting state laws

SUPREMACY CLAUSE
Supremacy Clause
•
Constitution, U.S. laws, U.S. treaties supersede conflicting state and local laws
Express Preemption
•
Expressly providing state and local governments may not legislate within
particular area

Implied Preemption
Conflict Preemption
•
Congressional act or federal law preempts conflicting state or local
law
Object Preemption
•
State law obstructs federal law’s objective
Field Preemption
•
Congress demonstrates intent to occupy entire field
CONTRACTS CLAUSE
Contracts Clause
•
Prevents state legislation retroactively impairing obligation of Ks
Private Contracts
•
Government’s impairment must be rx and necessary to achieve important
government purpose
Public Contracts
•
Government’s impairment must be rx and necessary to achieve important
government purpose that could not be accomplished through less restrictive
means
PRIVILEGES AND IMMUNITIES
Fourteenth Amendment Privileges and Immunities
•
States may not intentionally discriminate against out-of-state residents
•
Discriminatory state laws valid if
(1) Out-of-state residents are source of problem government attempting to resolve,

and
(2) No less restrictive means exist to achieve important government interest
DORMANT COMMERCE CLAUSE

Dormant Commerce Clause
•
State and local laws may regulate interstate commerce only if legislation does not (i)
directly discriminate against out-of-state commerce or (ii) unduly burden interstate
commerce

blocks.text:
Discrimination
•
Law that discriminates against out-of-state commerce is presumed invalid
unless
(1) Law furthers important state interest, and
(2) No other non-discriminator alternatives exist
Exceptions
Market Participant
•
State hiring, selling, or purchasing goods
Express Congressional Approval
•
Congress may permit states to enact legislation that
discriminates against out-of-state commerce
Unduly Burden to Interstate Commerce
•
Non-discriminatory law may still be invalid if places undue burden on
interstate commerce
TAKINGS CLAUSE
Takings Clause
•
Prohibits government from taking private property for public use without just compensation
Property Interest
•
Must have real property interest to challenge

Taking
Possessory or Per Se
Physical Occupation
•
Legislation → Permanent physical government occupation
Constructive Occupation
•
Legislation → Property’s value or use is impaired
Regulatory
•
Government places regulations such that owner is deprived of benefit of his
property
Public Use
•
Rationally related to conceivable public purpose
•
Rx belief the public will benefit

blocks.text:
Just Compensation
•
FMV of property at time of taking
DUE PROCESS
PROCEDURAL DUE PROCESS
Procedural Due Process
•
No state shall make or enforce any law, which shall deprive any person of life,
liberty, or property without due process of law
Threatened Protected Interest
Property
•
Legitimate claim
Liberty
•
Deprived of physical freedom, freedom of choice, or fundamental
rights

Process Due
•
Factors
(i) Private interest affected
(ii) Risk of erroneous deprivation of interest through procedures used and

probable value of additional procedural safeguards
(iii) Fiscal and administrative burden in providing additional process
Notice and Hearing
•
Generally entitled to notice and hearing
SUBSTANTIVE DUE PROCESS
Substantive Due Process
•
Protects against unlawful impingement of rights
Standards of Review
Fundamental Rights
•
Fundamental right → Strict scrutiny
Strict Scrutiny
(1) Necessary and least restrictive means
(2) Compelling government interest

blocks.text:
Non-Fundamental Rights
•
Non-fundamental right → Rational basis
Rational Basis
(1) Rationally related
(2) Legitimate government interest
FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS
Fundamental Rights
•
Right to travel
•
Right to vote
•
Right to privacy
•
Right to family autonomy
•
Marriage, divorce, custody, child-rearing
•
Right to reproductive autonomy
•
Procreation, sexual relations, contraception, abortion
•
Right to refuse medical treatment
Abortion
Pre-Viability
•
May regulate abortion
•
Regulations must not place undue burden on right to abortion

Post-Viability
•
May regulate once fetus reaches viability
•
Must provide exception to preserve health or life of mother
EQUAL PROTECTION
Equal Protection Clause
•
All persons entitled to equal protection of the laws
Discriminatory Classification
Facially Discriminatory
•
Creates distinctions between categories of people on its face
Facially Neutral Law with Discriminatory Application
•
Creates disparate impact when applied

blocks.text:
Symbolic Speech
•
May regulate if
(1) Regulation furthers important government interest
(2) Government interest unrelated to suppression of speech, and
(3) Regulation burdens speech no greater than necessary
Content-Based Regulation
•
Restrictions based on content
•
Strict scrutiny
Content-Neutral Regulation
•
Restrictions on all speech
•
Intermediate scrutiny
Unprotected and Lesser-Protected Speech
Obscenity
(1) Appeals to prurient interest
(2) Depicts sexual conduct in patently offensive way, and
(3) Lacks serious literary, artistic, political, scientific value
Incitement
(1) Directed at inciting imminent lawless action, and
(2) Likely to incite such action
Fighting Words
•
Inflicts injury or incites immediate breach of peace or violence
Commercial Speech
•
Protected unless false or misleading
•
Regulations must
(1) Be narrowly tailored
(2) Directly advance
(3) Substantial government interest

blocks.text:
FORUM
Public Forums
•
Traditional: Historically used for expressive activities
•
Limited: Designated for public expressive purpose

Content-Neutral
Time, Place, Manner
•
Intermediate scrutiny
(1) Narrowly tailored
(2) Achieve important government interest, and
(3) Leaves open ample alternative means
Content-Based
•
Strict scrutiny
Non-Public Forum
(1) Viewpoint-neutral, and
(2) Satisfies rational basis test
LIMITATIONS
Vagueness
•
Rx person cannot determine whether speech is prohibited or permitted
Overbreadth
•
Regulates substantially more speech than Constitution allows
Prior Restraint
•
Restricts harmful speech before it’s expressed
•
Strict scrutiny
FREEDOM OF RELIGION
ESTABLISHMENT CLAUSE
Establishment Clause
•
Government may not establish official religion, favor one religion over
another, or favor religion over non-religion
Facially Religious Preference
•
Prefers one religion over another on its face
•
Strict scrutiny

blocks.text:
No Facially Religious Preference – Lemon Test
•
Law’s impact favors a religion
Lemon Test
(1) Predominant secular purpose
(2) Primary effect neither advances nor inhibits religion, and
(3) No excessive government entanglement with religion
FREE EXERCISE OF RELIGION

Free Exercise Clause
•
Government may not prohibit free exercise of religion
•
Strict scrutiny

Religious Belief
•
May look to one’s sincerity in asserting belief
Laws Targeting Religious Conduct
•
Strict scrutiny

Laws of General Applicability
•
Rational basis test
FREEDOM OF ASSOCIATION
Freedom of Association
•
Government must not penalize person affiliated with a group unless
(1) Group actively involved in unlawful activity
(2) Person had knowledge of activity, and
(3) Intent to advance activity

Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
•
Ks involving sale of goods
Mixed Contracts – Predominant Purpose Test
•
Ks involving both services and sale of goods, courts determine K’s predominant purpose
CONTRACT FORMATION

Formation
(1) Mutual assent
(2) Consideration, and
(3) No defenses to formation
Offer
(1) Manifest intent to contract
(2) Definite and certain terms, and
(3) Communication of offer to offeree

Intent
•
Words or conduct must show present intent
Definite and Certain Terms

Common Law
•
Requires all essential terms
•
Parties
•
Purpose
•
Quantity
•
Cost
UCC
•
Only essential term is quantity
Communication to the Offeree
•
Offer must be communicated to offeree

    ✷  Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
           •  Ks involving sale of goods

    ✷  Mixed Contracts – Predominant Purpose Test
           •  Ks involving both services and sale of goods, courts determine K’s predominant purpose

             (1) Mutual assent
             (2) Consideration, and
             (3) No defenses to formation

    ✷  Offer
             (1) Manifest intent to contract
             (2) Definite and certain terms, and
             (3) Communication of offer to offeree

        ✷  Intent
                  •  Words or conduct must show present intent

        ✷  Definite and Certain Terms

            ✷ Common Law
                         •   Requires all essential terms
                            •   Parties
                            •   Purpose

                            •   Cost

blocks.text:
Advertisements
•
Considered an offer if
(i)  Clear and definite terms and limits who may accept, or
(ii) Includes stated reward
TERMINATION OF OFFER

Termination
•
Offer can be terminated prior to acceptance

Direct
•
Expressly communicating revocation to offeree
•
Effective upon receipt
Indirect
(1) Conduct takes definite action inconsistent with intent to contract,

and
(2) Offeree knows about conduct from reliable source
Rejection
•
Effective upon receipt
Counteroffer
•
Operates as rejection and creates new offer

Lapse of Time
•
Terminated if not accepted within rx time or by deadline
Death or Mental Incapacity
•
Terminated if offeror dies or loses capacity before acceptance
Destruction of Illegality
•
Terminated if subject matter destroyed or illegal
Irrevocable Offers

Option Contracts
•
Consideration paid to offeror to keep offer open for specified time
UCC – Firm Offer
(1) Offer by merchant to buy or sell goods
(2) Signed writing, and
(3) Assures offer held open for time stated, not to exceed 3 months

blocks.text:
Promissory Estoppel
•
Offeree detrimentally relies on offer
•
Reliance rxly fx
Partial Performance

Unilateral Contracts
•
Accepted by rendering performance
Bilateral Contracts
•
Accepted by promise or performance
ACCEPTANCE OF OFFER

Acceptance
•
Objective manifestation of assent to terms

Unilateral Contracts
•
Accepted upon completion of performance
Bilateral Contracts
•
Accepted by promise or starting performance
Means of Acceptance

Silence
•
Not valid unless
(1) History of silence, and
(2) Offeror has given offeree reason to believe he may accept by silence, and

offeree, in remaining silent, intends to accept
UCC – Shipment of Goods
•
Accepted by promise to ship or shipment of goods

UCC – Non-Conforming Goods
•
Seller ships non-conforming goods → Offer accepted and K
breached
Mailbox Rule
•
Acceptance effective upon dispatch

Exceptions – Mailbox Rule
•
Offeree sends rejection first, but then sends acceptance → Communication
received first controls
•
Offeror’s revocation effective upon receipt
•
Option Ks
•
Firm offers

blocks.text:
Mirror Image Rule
•
Acceptance must mirror the offer
•
Additional or different terms creates counteroffer
UCC – Battle of the Forms
•
Additional or different terms generally considered acceptance

UCC – Both Parties are Merchants
•
One or both parties not merchants → New terms deemed proposals,
become part of K upon other party’s approval

UCC – Additional Terms
• Both parties merchants → New terms become part of K
unless
(i)  Materially alter K
(ii) Offer expressly limits acceptance to terms, or
 (ii) Offeror objects within rx time
UCC – Conflicting Terms
•
Conflicting terms cancel each other out
CONSIDERATION

Consideration
• Bargained-for exchange of legal detriment or benefit
Sufficient Consideration

UCC – Requirement Contracts
•
Seller agrees to supply buyer with as much of item as buyer needs

UCC – Output Contracts
•
Seller agrees to sell entire production of item to buyer

Promise Not to Sue
•
P must have good faith belief of valid claim

Preexisting Duty
•
Not adequate consideration unless
(i)  New or different consideration
(ii) 3P’s promise based upon performance of original K, or
(iii) Unexpected circumstances

blocks.text:
Illusory Promise
•
Unenforceable due to indefiniteness and option of whether to be bound
Gifts
•
Unenforceable
Past Consideration
•
Unenforceable
Consideration Substitutes

Promissory Estoppel
(1) Promisor rxly expects promise to induce other party to act or refrain from

acting
(2) Other party relies on promise and acts in accordance with reliance, and
(3) Injustice cannot be avoided unless promise enforced
DEFENSES TO CONTRACT FORMATION

Mutual Mistake
•
Both parties mistaken about essential element of K
•
May reform or rescind K if
(1) Mistake present at time of K formation
(2) Mistake pertained to material assumption, and
(3) Party did not assume risk
Unilateral Mistake
•
One party mistaken about essential element of K
•
May avoid K if
(1) Mistake present at time of K formation, and
(2) Mistake pertained to material assumption
(3) Party did not assume risk, and
(4) Mistake would make K unconscionable

Misrepresentation
(1) Fraudulent or material
(2) Relied upon for assent, and
(3) Justified reliance
Incapacity
•
Capacity required to contract, otherwise K voidable

blocks.text:
Necessities
•
Incapacitated individuals required to pay fair value
Misunderstanding
(1) Material term in K can have 2+ interpretations
(2) Each party interpreted term differently, and
(3) Either (i) neither party aware, or (ii) both parties aware of misunderstanding

Duress
•
Improper threat → Deprive free will and induce to contract
DEFENSES TO CONTRACT ENFORCEMENT

Illegality
•
K unenforceable
Unconscionability
•
Courts may
(i)  Refuse to enforce K
(ii) Enforce remainder of K without unconscionable term, or
(iii) Limit application to avoid unconscionable result

Procedural Unconscionability
•
Surprise or unequal bargaining power
Substantive Unconscionability
•
Substance of K → One-sided results
Statute of Frauds
(1) Writing
(2) Signed by party to be charged, and
(3) Essential elements of transaction
Contracts Within the Statute of Frauds

Suretyship
•
Promise to pay debt of another

Real Property
•
Create interest in real property for 1+ year

Exception – Part Performance
•
SOF satisfied if at least 2 of following occurred
(i) Possession
(ii) Payment

blocks.text:
(iii) Improvements
One Year
•
Cannot be fully performed within 1 year
UCC – Sale of Goods
•
Sale of goods for $500+

UCC – Exception – Merchants Confirmatory Memo
•
If both parties merchants, writing sent by party within rx
time to confirm K unless objection within 10 days of receipt
Exceptions – Statute of Frauds

Promissory Estoppel
(1) Promisor rxly expects promise to induce other party to act or refrain

from acting
(2) Other party relies on promise and acts in accordance with reliance,

and
(3) Injustice cannot be avoided unless promise enforced
Judicial Acknowledgment
•
Party admits to existence of K in pleadings or testimony
Frustration of Purpose
(1) Both parties know K’s purpose at time of formation
(2) Purpose becomes frustrated through no fault of party, and
(3) Purpose becomes frustrated due to unexpected supervening event outside party’s

control
Impossibility
•
Unforeseen circumstances after K formation → Performance impossible
Impracticability
(1) Occurrence of unfx event after K formation
(2) Event not caused by party seeking discharge
(3) Neither party anticipated event at time of formation, and
(4) Performance unrxly difficult

Condition Precedent
•
Must be satisfied before obligation to perform arises

Condition Subsequent
•
Occurrence will discharge other party’s duty to perform
Concurrent Conditions
•
One party’s performance predicated upon other’s
Satisfaction of Conditions

Objective Standard
•
Deemed satisfied if rx person would be satisfied
Subjective Standard – Aesthetic Taste
•
Subjective standard
•
Dissatisfaction must be in good faith
Waiver of Conditions
•
Party may waive condition prior to satisfaction and retract waiver unless
other party detrimentally relied
Parol Evidence Rule
•
Evidence of communications prior to or contemporaneous with K inadmissible for
purpose of contradicting terms

Total Integration
•
Parol evidence inadmissible
Partial Integration
•
Parties can introduce evidence consistent with terms of K

UCC
•
Presumes all writings to be partially integrated
Exceptions – Parol Evidence Rule
•
Admissible to
(i)  Clarify ambiguity
(ii) Prove condition precedent
(iii) Raise defense to K formation
(iv) Resolve mistake
(v) Establish other K

UCC
•
Evidence of course of dealing, usage of trade, course of performance may be
introduced to explain or supplement terms of K

blocks.text:
Common Law
•
Independent consideration required unless modification
(i)  Is fair due to unanticipated circumstances, or
(ii) Creates new K obligations for each party
UCC
•
Independent consideration not required as long as good faith
UCC – Warranties

UCC – Express Warranty
•
Description of goods, affirmation of fact, promise made by seller to buyer

UCC – Express Warranty – Disclaimer
•
Invalid if unrxly negates or limits
UCC – Implied Warranty of Merchantability
•
Merchantability of goods implied in K if seller is merchant with respect to
goods of that kind

UCC – Implied Warranty of Merchantability – Disclaimer
•
Language: “As is,” “with all faults,” etc.
•
Oral or written
•
Must expressly use “merchantability”
UCC – Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose
•
Seller has reason to know buyer relying on expertise to select good for
particular purpose

UCC – Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose – Disclaimer
•
Must be conspicuous and written
UCC – PERFORMANCE OF SALE OF GOODS CONTRACT

UCC – Perfect Tender Rule
•
Requires seller to render perfect goods and perfect delivery
•
Any non-conformity constitutes breach

UCC – Conforming Tender
•
Buyer has duty to accept goods and pay for them
•
Rejection is effectively a breach
UCC – Non-Conforming Tender – Buyer’s Right of Rejection
•
Non-conformity is breach
•
Buyer may

blocks.text:
(i)  Reject whole
(ii) Accept whole, or
(iii) Accept any commercial unit and reject rest
UCC – Exceptions – Perfect Tender Rule

UCC – Seller’s Right to Cure
•
If time for performance has not expired and seller notified buyer of intention
to cure
UCC – Installment Contracts
•
Perfect Tender Rule not applicable
•
Buyer may reject only if non-conformity substantially impairs value and time
to cure expired
UCC – Risk of Loss

UCC – Shipment Contract
•
Seller must
(1) Put goods in possession of common carrier and make rx K for

transportation
(2) Arrange for delivery, and
(3) Notify buyer of shipment
•
Risk of loss shifts to buyer

UCC – Destination Contract
•
Seller must deliver goods to destination in K
•
Seller bears risk of loss
THIRD-PARTY ISSUES

Intended Beneficiary
•
2 parties enter into K with mutual understanding and intent that K benefits 3P

Enforcement of Rights
•
Can sue to enforce K only if rights vested
Incidental Beneficiary
•
Party benefits from K even though parties did not intend for him to benefit
•
Cannot sue to enforce K
ASSIGNMENT OF RIGHTS

Assignment
•
Assignee assumes all rights and obligations and is subject to same defenses as
assignor

blocks.text:
Revocability
•
Consideration not required
•
If consideration, assignment irrevocable
Exceptions
•
Not allowed if
(i)  Materially alters duty or risk
(ii) Forbidden by law, or
(iii) Forbidden by K provision
Multiple Assignments
•
Last assignment revokes any preexisting revocable assignment
DELEGATION OF DUTIES

Delegation
•
Party may delegate duties to 3P
•
Delegator remains liable under K

Exceptions
•
Not allowed if
(i)  Non-delegating party’s personal skill or judgment required
(ii) Forbidden by law, or
(iii) Forbidden by K provision
Novation
•
All parties agree to replace delegator with delegatee
•
Delegator released from liability under K
BREACH OF CONTRACT

Material Breach
•
Nonbreaching party does not obtain substantial benefit of bargain because
other party failed to perform
•
Excuses nonbreaching party’s performance

Time is of the Essence Clause
•
Failure to perform by specific date not material breach unless time is
of the essence clause
Minor Breach
•
Party substantially performed under K
•
Does not excuse nonbreaching party’s performance

blocks.text:
Minor Breach with Anticipatory Repudiation
•
Minor breach + anticipatory repudiation → Material breach

UCC – Perfect Tender Rule
See above for Perfect Tender Rule.
ANTICIPATORY REPUDIATION

Anticipatory Repudiation
•
Unequivocal manifestation of unwillingness or inability to perform prior to time of
performance

Nonbreaching Party’s Options
(i) Sue immediately
(ii) Suspend own performance and wait until performance date to sue, or
(iii) Urge repudiating party to perform

Retraction
•
May retract unless
(i)  Materially change position based upon reliance
(ii) Accept repudiation, or
(iii) File COA for breach

Adequate Assurances
•
Rx grounds for insecurity re other party’s performance, may suspend own
performance and demand adequate assurances in writing

UCC
•
Must be provided within 30 days

Accord and Satisfaction
•
Accord: Promise to accept stated performance in satisfaction of existing duty
•
Satisfaction: Accord K performed

Breach
•
May enforce original K or accord K
REMEDIES

Duty to Mitigate
•
P has duty to mitigate damages

blocks.text:
•
Recovery reduced for failing to mitigate

Expectation Damages
•
Result directly from breach of K
•
Put P in position he would have been in had K been performed

Consequential Damages
•
Result indirectly from breach of K
•
Damages
(1) Fx at time of K formation
(2) Caused by D’s breach, and
(3) Calculated with rx certainty
Incidental Damages
•
Rxly incurred expenses by P to remedy breach of K

Reliance Damages
•
Alternative to expectation damages
•
Expenditures made in fx reliance of K
•
Put P in position he would have been in had K never existed
Liquidated Damages
•
Specified at K formation
•
Rx estimate of actual damages
Nominal Damages
•
Elements of COA established but not substantial loss
Punitive Damages
•
Generally unavailable
NON-MONETARY DAMAGES

Specific Performance
•
D ordered to perform K
(1) Valid K exists
(2) P performed or is able to perform
(3) Monetary damages inadequate
(4) Feasibility of court’s enforcement, and
(5) No defenses exist

blocks.text:
Plaintiff’s Performance
Monetary Damages Inadequate
•
Inadequate when (i) rare or unique personal property or (ii) real property
Court’s Enforcement
•
Specific performance not ordered if (i) extensive supervision or (ii)
enforcement too difficult
No Defenses
•
No specific performance if D asserts equitable defenses

Laches
(1) P unrxly delayed filing COA, and
(2) Delay causes prejudice to D
Unclean Hands
•
P engaged in unethical, illegal, or improper conduct
Quasi-Contract
(1) No valid K
(2) P conferred benefit upon D
(3) P rxly expected to get paid
(4) D asked for benefit, and
(5) D unjustly enriched if allowed to retain benefit

Replevin
•
Recover personal property wrongfully taken by D
Ejectment
•
Recover real property wrongfully possessed by D
Reformation
•
Re-writing of K to accurately reflect parties’ original intent

Mutual Mistake
•
Both parties mistaken about essential element of K
Unilateral Mistake
•
One party mistaken about essential element of K
Recission
•
Cancels K due to invalid formation
•
P must
(1) Give notice, and
(2) Return benefits received from D

(3) P rxly expected to get paid
(4) D asked for benefit, and
(5) D unjustly enriched if allowed to retain benefit

Replevin
•  Recover personal property wrongfully taken by D

Ejectment
•  Recover real property wrongfully possessed by D

Reformation
•   Re-writing of K to accurately reflect parties’ original intent

       Mutual Mistake
       •  Both parties mistaken about essential element of K

        Unilateral Mistake
       •  One party mistaken about essential element of K

Recission
•   Cancels K due to invalid formation
•  P must
    (1) Give notice, and
    (2) Return benefits received from D




                            14                                          Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                     sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text: UCC – Buyer’s Remedies for Failure to Tender Goods

UCC – Damages
•
(Market price – K price) + (incidental and/or consequential damages) –
(expenses saved)
UCC – Cover
•
(Cost of cover – K Price) + (incidental and/or consequential damages) –
(expenses saved)
Specific Performance
See above for Specific Performance.
UCC – Seller’s Remedies

UCC – Right to Price
•
May recover full K price if
(i)  Goods accepted
(ii) Conforming goods lost or damaged, or
(iii) Seller unable, after rx effort, to resell goods
UCC – Buyer’s Insolvency
•
May refuse to deliver goods
•
May stop delivery in transit

UCC – Credit
•
If discover good received on credit while insolvent, may reclaim
goods upon demand within 10 days after receipt
UCC – Wrongful Rejection by Buyer
•
Seller may
(i)  Withhold or stop delivery
(ii) Resell goods and recover damages
(iii) Recover damages for non-acceptance, or
(iv) Cancel K
UCC – Lost Volume Seller
•
Seller would have been able to sell to both breaching buyer and
buyer to which goods were resold
•
Entitled to profit which seller would have made from full
performance by buyer + any incidental damages

blocks.text:
Promoters
•
Raise capital and enter into Ks to form corporation

Liability
•
Personally liable for all pre-incorporation Ks unless (i) novation or (ii) adoption

Novation
•
Parties agree to replace promoter with corporation in K
Adoption
•
Express: Per board resolution
•
Implied: Accepting benefits of K
INCORPORATION

Procedure
•
Incorporator must file articles of incorporation with Secretary of State
•
Articles of incorporation must include
•
Name
•
Purpose
•
Number of shares authorized to issue
•
Agent’s name and address
•
Incorporator’s name and address

Ultra Vires
•
Corporation transacts beyond scope of specified narrow purpose

Challenging Ultra Vires
•
Challenge to enjoin ultra vires act can be brought by (i) shareholder against
corporation, (ii) corporation against director, officer, employee, or agent, or
(iii) state
De Jure Corporation
•
Validly incorporated corporation
De Facto Corporation
•
Operating corporation without knowledge that good faith attempt to incorporate was
unsuccessful
Corporation by Estoppel
(1) Operating corporation without knowledge that good faith attempt to incorporate was

unsuccessful, and
(2) 3P contract with owner believing it to be de jure corporation

•
Prevents 3P from (i) denying corporation’s existence or (ii) holding owner personally
liable

CORPORATIONS

PRE-INCORPORATION MATTERS

  ✷  Promoters
       •   Raise capital and enter into Ks to form corporation

      ✷  Liability
              •   Personally liable for all pre-incorporation Ks unless (i) novation or (ii) adoption

          ✷  Novation
                     •   Parties agree to replace promoter with corporation in K

          ✷  Adoption
                     •   Express: Per board resolution
                     •   Implied: Accepting benefits of K

INCORPORATION

       Procedure
       •   Incorporator must file articles of incorporation with Secretary of State
       •   Articles of incorporation must include
           •  Name
           •   Purpose
           •  Number of shares authorized to issue
           •   Agent’s name and address
           •   Incorporator’s name and address

                Ultra Vires
              •   Corporation transacts beyond scope of specified narrow purpose

                      Challenging Ultra Vires
                     •   Challenge to enjoin ultra vires act can be brought by (i) shareholder against
                            corporation, (ii) corporation against director, officer, employee, or agent, or
                                      (iii) state

  ✷ De Jure Corporation
       •   Validly incorporated corporation

  ✷ De Facto Corporation
       •   Operating corporation without knowledge that good faith attempt to incorporate was
           unsuccessful

  ✷  Corporation by Estoppel
        (1) Operating corporation without knowledge that good faith attempt to incorporate was
            unsuccessful, and
        (2) 3P contract with owner believing it to be de jure corporation
           •   Prevents 3P from (i) denying corporation’s existence or (ii) holding owner personally
                  liable

blocks.text:
Bylaws
•
Set out corporation’s governing rules
CORPORATION MANAGEMENT STRUCTURE

Board of Directors
•
Manage corporation
•
At least one director required
Selection
•
Elected by shareholders
Removal
•
Shareholders may remove directors through majority vote
Meetings
•
Regular: According to bylaws, no notice required
•
Special: Notice required unless waived by signed waiver or attendance

Voting
•
Quorum of directors must be in attendance for board’s actions to be valid
•
Resolutions require majority vote

Action Without Meeting
•
Unanimous written consent required
Officers

Officers
•
Carry out daily operations
•
Selected by board of directors

Express Actual Authority
•
Created through oral or written words
Implied Actual Authority
•
Based on officer’s rx understanding of corporation’s
objective

Implied Authority by Position
•
Based on officer’s job title or position
Apparent Authority
•
Corporation causes 3P’s rx belief that officer has authority to act

blocks.text:
Third Party’s Reasonable Belief
•
Based on trade customs, industry standards, and officer’s
position
Ratification
(1) Corporation expressly or impliedly demonstrates assent to K
(2) Must be timely
(3) Parties have capacity, and
(4) Corporation knows material facts
FIDUCIARY DUTIES OF DIRECTORS AND OFFICERS

Duty of Care
•
Directors and officers must act with care of rxly prudent person in similar position
•
Otherwise, may be personally liable

Reliance
•
May rxly rely on advice of others
Business Judgment Rule
•
Rebuttable presumption
•
Decision made (1) in good faith (2) with care of rxly prudent person and (3)
with rx belief that actions were in corporation’s best interest
Duty of Loyalty
•
Directors and officers must prioritize corporation’s interests above own

Self-Dealing
•
Director or officer transacts with a corporation for benefit of himself or his
relatives

Safe-Harbor Rule
•
Self-dealing transactions protected if approved or fair to corporation
Usurping a Corporate Opportunity
•
Directors and officers must disclose all corporate opportunities
•
May only personally pursue opportunity after rejected by corporation
SHAREHOLDERS

Shareholders
•
Elect board of directors
•
Vote on significant decisions
Meetings

blocks.text:
•
Main purpose: Elect directors
Special Meetings
•
Notice required – time, date, location, purpose
Voting
•
Each share of stock is entitled to 1 vote

Quorum
•
Quorum of shares must be in attendance for shareholders’ decision to be valid
Record Date
•
Only shareholders who hold shares on record date are entitled to vote
Proxy Voting
•
Shareholders may vote by proxy
•
Generally revocable but may be irrevocable if coupled with interest or expressly
stated
Voting Agreements
•
Multiple shareholders may enter into enforceable signed agreement for how to vote
their shares
Shareholder Litigation

Direct Lawsuits
•
Shareholder personally suing corporation
Derivative Lawsuits
•
Shareholder with standing suing on corporation’s behalf

Standing
•  Shareholder has standing if (1) owns shares at time of harm and throughout
      litigation and (2) adequately represents corporation’s interests

Written Demand
•
Prior to derivative lawsuit, (1) shareholder must make written
demand for board to file, and (2) demand must be rejected
Fiduciary Duties
•
Controlling shareholders: Owed to corporation and minority shareholders
•
Minority shareholders: None
Piercing the Corporate Veil

Factors
(i)   Undercapitalization
(ii)   Alter ego
(iii)   Fraud
(iv)   Siphoning corporate funds and stripping corporate assets
