(v)   Failure to observe formalities
(vi)   Self-dealing
(vii) Commingling
STOCK

Stock
•
Correlates to owner’s rights and interests
•
Common stock required
•
Board of directors approve issuance of stock
Valuation
•
Adequate consideration required in exchange for shares of stock
•
Adequacy determined by board of directors

Par Value
•
Minimum amount for which share of stock may be sold
•
Set par value not required
Watered Stock
•
Stock sold below par value

Liability
•
Board of directors: Liable for difference between par value and value
received
•
Shareholders: Liable if knowingly received watered stock
Distribution

Dividend
•
Distribution of profits to shareholders
Authority to Distribute
•
Authorized by board of directors
Federal Securities Laws

Rule 10b-5
•
Prohibits use of fraud to effectuate purchase or sale of securities

Claim Requirements
(1) P purchased or sold security
(2) Using interstate commerce
(3) D intentionally or recklessly
(4) Engaged in fraudulent conduct
(5) Pertaining to material info
(6) Upon which P relied, and
(7) Suffered harm

blocks.text:
Rule 16(b)
•
Obstructs unethical use of insider info for purchase and sale of securities
•
Applicable to
•
Directors, officers, shareholders owning more than 10%
•
Public corporation traded on national exchange
•
Corporation with assets > $10 million and a minimum of 2,000 shareholders
FUNDAMENTAL CORPORATE CHANGES

Dissolution
•
Corporation ceases to exist
•
Voluntary or involuntary action

Voluntary
•
Dissolved by directors and shareholders through majority vote
Involuntary

Creditors
•
Dissolved by creditors upon showing of insolvency
Shareholders
•
Dissolved by shareholders upon showing directors engaged in fraud or assets
are being wasted
Winding Up
•
After dissolution, corporation exists only to wind up and liquidate

pages.normalized_text:
✷  Rule 16(b)
              •   Obstructs unethical use of insider info for purchase and sale of securities
              •   Applicable to
                  •   Directors, officers, shareholders owning more than 10%
                  •   Public corporation traded on national exchange
                  •   Corporation with assets > $10 million and a minimum of 2,000 shareholders

FUNDAMENTAL CORPORATE CHANGES

  ✷  Dissolution
       •   Corporation ceases to exist
       •   Voluntary or involuntary action

      ✷  Voluntary
              •   Dissolved by directors and shareholders through majority vote

      ✷  Involuntary

          ✷  Creditors
                     •   Dissolved by creditors upon showing of insolvency

          ✷  Shareholders
                     •   Dissolved by shareholders upon showing directors engaged in fraud or assets
                            are being wasted

       Winding Up
       •   After dissolution, corporation exists only to wind up and liquidate

              •   Applicable to
                  •   Directors, officers, shareholders owning more than 10%
                  •   Public corporation traded on national exchange
                  •   Corporation with assets > $10 million and a minimum of 2,000 shareholders

FUNDAMENTAL CORPORATE CHANGES

  ✷  Dissolution
       •   Corporation ceases to exist
       •   Voluntary or involuntary action

      ✷  Voluntary
              •   Dissolved by directors and shareholders through majority vote

      ✷  Involuntary

          ✷  Creditors
                     •   Dissolved by creditors upon showing of insolvency

          ✷  Shareholders
                     •   Dissolved by shareholders upon showing directors engaged in fraud or assets
                            are being wasted

       Winding Up
       •   After dissolution, corporation exists only to wind up and liquidate





                                          6                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

Lesser-included crimes merge into greater crimes
•

Kidnapping

Common Law Murder
•

•
Intent to Kill
•
Intent to Inflict Great Bodily Injury

Reckless Indifference or Depraved Heart Murder
•
Felony Murder
Felony Murder
•
Unintended yet fx killing actually or proximately caused by
attempted commission of an inherently dangerous felony
Death of a Co-Felon
•
D not liable if co-felon killed by victim or police
Agency Theory
•
Felon liable for death caused by his agent
Point of Safety
•
Commission of felony concludes when D reaches point of
safety
First-Degree Murder
•
Premeditated and deliberate
Premeditated
• Enough time to reflect on killing
Deliberate
• Cool and dispassionate manner
Second-Degree Murder
•
Murder committed without premeditation and deliberation
Voluntary Manslaughter
(1) Provocation causing sudden and intense passion in ordinary rx person
(2) D was actually provoked
(3) Insufficient time for rx person to cool off, and
(4) D did not cool off

CRIMES AGAINST PROPERTY
Larceny
(1) Trespassory taking and
(2) Carrying away of
(3) Tangible personal property of another, and
(4) Intent to permanently deprive
Trespassory Taking
•
D exercises control over owner’s property without consent
Carrying Away
•
Requires movement
Personal Property of Another
•
Must be tangible personal property of another
With Intent to Permanently Deprive
•
Concurrence of taking and specific intent to permanently deprive
Robbery
(1) Larceny involving
(2) Use of force or intimidation to take property
(3) From another’s person or presence
Force or Intimidation
•
Violence, threats, or fear of imminent physical injury or death
Person or Presence
•
Take within victim’s person, presence, or control
Receiving Stolen Property
(1) Receipt of
(2) Stolen personal property
(3) Knowledge that property was stolen, and
(4) Intent to permanently deprive
Burglary
(1) Breaking
(2) Entering
(3) Dwelling
(4) Of another
(5) At night, and
(6) Specific intent to commit felony therein

•

Common law: Dwelling must belong to another
•
Modern law: Ownership is insignificant

blocks.text:
Solicitation Defenses
Renunciation
•
Common law: Not a defense
•
Model Penal Code: may be a defense if D also prevents commission of
solicited crime
Factual Impossibility
•
Not a defense
Conspiracy
(1) Agreement between 2 or more people with
(2) Specific intent to enter into agreement
(3) Specific intent to achieve criminal objective, and
(4) Overt act
Agreement
•
Can be either explicit or implicit
Specific Intent
•
To agree and to achieve criminal objective
Overt Act
•
Common law: Not required
•
Modern law: Requires overt act in furtherance of conspiracy
Merger Doctrine
•
Inapplicable to Conspiracy
Scope of Liability
Majority Rule
•
Conspirator can be liable for conspiracy and co-conspirator’s fx crimes in
furtherance of conspiracy
Minority Rule
•
Conspirator not liable for co-conspirator’s crimes unless he aids and abets in
commission
Conspiracy Defenses
Withdrawal
Liability for Conspiracy
•
Generally, not a defense
•
Model Penal Code: Can be a defense if D thwarts conspiracy’s success
Liability for Substantive Crimes
•
D may limit liability for co-conspirator’s crimes by
(i) Notifying conspiracy members of intent to withdraw, or

(ii) Timely contacting authorities
Attempt
(1) Specific intent to achieve unlawful result, and
(2) Substantial step
Specific Intent
•
Specific intent to commit a crime
Substantial Step
•
Substantial step toward committing the crime
Merger Doctrine
•
Applicable to Attempt
Attempt Defenses
Withdrawal
•
Not a defense
Factual Impossibility
•
Not a defense
Legal Impossibility
•
Complete defense
PARTY LIABILITY
Accomplice Liability
•
Accomplice liable for crime itself and all fx crimes
Accessory After-the-Fact
•
Accessory only liable for obstruction of justice, not principal’s committed crime
DEFENSES
Insanity
M’Naghten Rule
•
D not guilty if established that because of (1) defect of reason (2) due to mental
disease, D (3) did not know either the wrongfulness or nature and quality of act
Irresistible Impulse Test
•
D not guilty if unable to control conduct to conform to the law due to mental disease
Durham Rule
•
D not guilty if criminal act was product of mental disease or defect

              •  D not guilty if established that because of (1) defect of reason (2) due to mental
                     disease, D (3) did not know either the wrongfulness or nature and quality of act

                  Irresistible Impulse Test
              •  D not guilty if unable to control conduct to conform to the law due to mental disease

            Durham Rule
              •  D not guilty if criminal act was product of mental disease or defect

                                          7                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu
pages.raw_text:
(ii) Timely contacting authorities

  ✷  Attempt
        (1) Specific intent to achieve unlawful result, and
        (2) Substantial step

      ✷  Specific Intent
              •   Specific intent to commit a crime

      ✷  Substantial Step
              •   Substantial step toward committing the crime

      ✷  Merger Doctrine
              •   Applicable to Attempt

  ✷  Attempt Defenses

      ✷  Withdrawal
              •  Not a defense

      ✷  Factual Impossibility
              •  Not a defense

      ✷  Legal Impossibility
              •  Complete defense

PARTY LIABILITY

  ✷  Accomplice Liability
       •  Accomplice liable for crime itself and all fx crimes

       Accessory After-the-Fact
       •   Accessory only liable for obstruction of justice, not principal’s committed crime

DEFENSES

        Insanity

             M’Naghten Rule
              •  D not guilty if established that because of (1) defect of reason (2) due to mental
                     disease, D (3) did not know either the wrongfulness or nature and quality of act

                  Irresistible Impulse Test
              •  D not guilty if unable to control conduct to conform to the law due to mental disease

            Durham Rule
              •  D not guilty if criminal act was product of mental disease or defect

blocks.text:
Model Penal Code Test
•
D not guilty if lacked capacity to appreciate wrongfulness of act or conform conduct
to the law
Intoxication
Voluntary Intoxication
•
Voluntarily consuming intoxicating substance
Involuntary Intoxication
•
Involuntarily consuming intoxicating substance
(i) Without knowledge of its nature
(ii) Under duress, or
(iii) Pursuant to prescription or medical advice
Mistake
Mistake of Fact
•
Must negate requisite state of mind
Mistake of Law
•
D unaware that actions are unlawful
Entrapment
(1) Criminal activity induced by law enforcement, and
(2) D not otherwise predisposed
JUSTIFICATIONS
Self-Defense
Non-Deadly Force
•
Rx force OK to protect against imminent danger or injury unless initial aggressor
Deadly Force
•
Deadly force OK to protect against death or great bodily harm
•
Majority: No duty to retreat
•
Minority: Duty to retreat if may be accomplished safely
Imperfect Self-Defense
•
Unrx self-defense and unjustifiably killing another
•
Must be used in good faith
Defense of Others
•
Using same amount of force that one rxly believes victim would be legally justified to use to
defend herself

blocks.text:
Fourth Amendment
•
Prohibits unrx searches and seizures
•
4A violation requires (1) government conduct and (2) standing

Government Conduct
•
Actions by government or its agents

Home
•
Always REOP where one resides, owns, possesses, or overnight
guest

Curtilage
•
REOP
•
Factors
(i) Proximity
(ii) Enclosure
(iii) Nature of use
(iv) Steps taken to protect from observation

Business Premises
•
REOP
•
Subject to administrative searches

Overheard Conversations
•
No REOP if naturally overheard

blocks.text:
Wiretapping and Recorded Calls
•
No REOP if one party consents

•
No REOP in info knowingly shared with 3Ps who may disclose

Inherently Public Objects and Characteristics
•
No REOP

Search
•
Violation of REOP
•
Must be rx and pursuant to valid warrant unless warrant exception applies

Fly Over
•
Not search if viewed from at least 400 ft in the air

Ladders
•
Not search even if used to view contents protected by REOP

Technology
•
Search if technology not used by general public

Canine Sniff
•
Search if accompanied with trespass

Validity of Search Warrant
(1) Issued by neutral and detached magistrate
(2) Based upon probable cause, and
(3) Describes with rx particularity

(i) Places to be searched
(ii) Things to be seized

Knock and Announce Rule
•
Must knock and announce presence when executing warrant

Exception
•
Not required if exigent circumstances or rx suspicion that
announcing would be dangerous or allow destruction of
evidence

Good Faith Exception to An Invalid Warrant
•
Evidence obtained not excluded if officer acted in good faith reliance

blocks.text:
Frisk of Persons
•
Once stopped, protective frisk for weapons allowed
•
Requires rx suspicion that person may be armed

Plain Feel Exception
•
During frisk, police may seize if rx belief item is contraband
•
Nature of item must be immediately obvious

Stop and Frisk of Automobiles
•
Stop: Requires rx suspicion of traffic violation
•
Frisk: Requires rx suspicion that weapon is inside vehicle, may only
frisk passenger compartment where weapons may be

Search Incident to a Lawful Arrest
•
If lawful arrest, may search arrestee

Scope of Search – Person
•
May search arrestee’s person and wingspan
Scope of Search – Homes
•
May search arrestee’s wingspan and conduct protective sweep where
accomplice may be hiding
Scope of Search – Automobiles
•
May search car only if arrestee is (i) unsecured and within reach of passenger
compartment or (ii) rx belief evidence contained therein

Automobile Exception
•
If probable cause that automobile contains contraband, may search entire vehicle

Plain View
•
May seize evidence in plain view if
(1) Police lawfully in location
(2) Nature of evidence immediately apparent, and
(3) Police may lawfully access item

Exigent Circumstances
•
Immediate action required
(i) Administer emergency aid
(ii) Prevent destruction of evidence
(iii) Hot pursuit

blocks.text:
Consent
•
Must have authority to give consent
•
Must be voluntary

Scope
•
Consenting party may limit scope or withdraw consent

Shared Property
•
D present: Co-occupant’s consent invalid
•
D not present: Co-occupant’s consent valid

Seizure of Persons
•
Rx person would not feel free to leave

Requirements
(1) Neutral and detached magistrate
(2) Based upon probable cause, and
(3) Crime committed by person identified in warrant

Arrest Within Arrestee’s Home
•
Warrant mandatory unless exigent circumstances or valid
consent
Arrest Within Third Party’s Home
•
Requires arrest warrant for suspect and search warrant for
3P’s home unless exigent circumstances or 3P’s valid
consent

Felonies Outside Officer’s Presence
•
Warrant not required if probable cause to believe felony
committed

Felonies and Misdemeanors Within Officer’s Presence
•
Warrant not required if felony or misdemeanor committed

Terry Stop and Frisk
See above under Warrant Exceptions.

Police Checkpoints
(1) Non-discriminatory in execution, and
(2) Articulable purpose other than investigation of criminal activity

blocks.text:
Exclusionary Rule
•
FOPT: Evidence gathered in violation of D’s 4A, 5A, or 6A rights,
•
FOPT may not be introduced at trial unless exception applies

Inevitable Discovery
•
Evidence would have been inevitably and lawfully discovered

Independent Source
•
Legal discovery through independent source

Attenuation of the Taint
•
Causal connection has been attenuated

Good Faith Reliance
•
Search or arrest pursuant to defective warrant but relied on in good faith

Invalid Reliance
(i) Warrant so deficient that no rx officer could have relied in good faith
(ii) Biased magistrate, or
(iii) Warrant secured through fraudulent means
FIFTH AMENDMENT

Fifth Amendment
•
No person shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself

Miranda Warnings
•
Must be provided prior to any custodial interrogation
(1) Right to remain silent
(2) Anything D says can be used against him in court of law
(3) Right to attorney during interrogation
(4) If he cannot afford attorney, one will be provided

Custody
•
Formally arrested or deprived of movement
•
Test: Whether a rx person in same circumstance would believe he is not free
       to leave
Interrogation
•
Express questioning likely to elicit incriminating response

Waiver of Miranda Rights
(1) Knowingly and intelligently made
(2) Voluntary, and
(3) Uncoerced

Invoking Miranda
•
Clear and unequivocal assertion required

blocks.text:
Right to Counsel
•
All questioning must cease until counsel is present unless suspect
re-instigates or at least 2 weeks have passed since release and fresh
Miranda warnings provided
Right to Silence
•
Must “scrupulously honor” unless suspect re-instigates
•
May still question re unrelated crimes

Exclusionary Rule and Miranda
•
Statements obtained in violation of Miranda
•
Subject to Exclusionary Rule
•
May be admitted for impeachment purposes
•
Not subject to FOPT doctrine
SIXTH AMENDMENT

Right to Assistance of Counsel
•
Attaches automatically once formal adversarial judicial proceedings begin
•
Remains throughout all critical stages
•
Ends at sentencing

Exclusionary Rule and Sixth Amendment
•
Subject to FOPT consequences unless Exclusionary Rule exception applies

Offense Specific
•
Applies to specific offenses that D has been formally charged with

Ineffective Assistance of Counsel
•
D may appeal case on basis of ineffective assistance of counsel

Waiver
(1) Knowingly and intelligently made, and
(2) Voluntary

Pro Se
•
D may waive right to counsel
(1) Knowingly and intelligently made, and
(2)  Voluntary

Right to a Speedy Trial
•
D has right to a speedy trial

✷  Right to Counsel
                     •   All questioning must cease until counsel is present unless suspect
                              re-instigates or at least 2 weeks have passed since release and fresh
                       Miranda warnings provided

          ✷  Right to Silence
                     •  Must “scrupulously honor” unless suspect re-instigates
                     •  May still question re unrelated crimes

              Voluntary Statements
              •  Not protected by 5A

      ✷  Exclusionary Rule and Miranda
              •   Statements obtained in violation of Miranda
                  •   Subject to Exclusionary Rule
                  •  May be admitted for impeachment purposes
                  •  Not subject to FOPT doctrine

SIXTH AMENDMENT

        Sixth Amendment

      ✷  Right to Assistance of Counsel
              •   Attaches automatically once formal adversarial judicial proceedings begin
              •  Remains throughout all critical stages
              •  Ends at sentencing

                      Exclusionary Rule and Sixth Amendment
                     •   Subject to FOPT consequences unless Exclusionary Rule exception applies

          ✷  Offense Specific
                     •   Applies to specific offenses that D has been formally charged with

          ✷   Ineffective Assistance of Counsel
                     •  D may appeal case on basis of ineffective assistance of counsel

          ✷  Waiver
                         (1) Knowingly and intelligently made, and
                         (2) Voluntary

              ✷  Pro Se
                            •  D may waive right to counsel
                                      (1) Knowingly and intelligently made, and
                                      (2) Voluntary

               Right to a Speedy Trial
              •  D has right to a speedy trial

                                          6                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

•
Factors
(i) Length of delay
(ii) Reason for delay
(iii) Whether D objected to delay
(iv) Prejudice to D

Right to a Jury Trial
•
D has right to jury trial for offenses that carry potential sentences for more than 6
months
Right to Testify
•
D has right to testify his own trial

Right to Confront Witnesses – Confrontation Clause
•
D has right to confront all witnesses testifying against him

•

•

action

•

unfair prejudice, confusing issues, misleading jury, wasting time, or cumulative
evidence

California – Proposition 8
•
Applicable to criminal proceedings
•
Relevant evidence excluded if probative value substantially outweighed by
danger of unfair prejudice, confusing issues, or misleading jury

•

O, R

blocks.text:
Defendant Attacks the Victim’s Character
•
D may offer O, R of victim’s pertinent character trait

California
•
D may offer SI, O, R of victim’s pertinent character trait
Prosecution’s Response
•
P may offer O, R of victim’s good character or D’s same
trait
Sex Offense Cases
•
Evidence of victim’s sexual predisposition inadmissible

Exceptions – Civil Cases
•
Evidence of victim’s sexual predisposition admissible if
(1) Probative value substantially outweighs unfair prejudice, and
(2) Victim put into issue first
•
SI, O, R
Exceptions – Criminal Cases
•
SI admissible to demonstrate
(i) 3P source of semen, injury, physical evidence
(ii) Past sexual relations

•
D: To prove consent
•
P: For any reason
(iii) Evidence whose exclusion would violate D’s rights

Specific Acts
•
Inadmissible to prove character and person acted in accordance with that character

Exceptions
•
Admissible to prove
(i)  Motive
(ii) Opportunity
(iii) Intent
(iv) Preparation
(v)  Plan
(vi) Knowledge
(vii) Identity
(viii) Absence of mistake
(ix)  Lack of accident

Evidence of Habit
•
Admissible to prove person or organization acted in accordance with habit or routine

blocks.text:
Impeachment
•
Admissible for impeachment
Methods of Proving Character
•
If admissible, may be proved by O or R
•
Cross-examination: May allow SI
TRIAL

Competency
•
May testify only if evidence sufficient to support finding that he has personal
knowledge of a matter
Lay Witnesses
•
O testimony admissible when
(1) Rationally based on witness’s perception
(2) Helpful to understand witness’s testimony or determine fact in issue, and
(3) Not based on specialized knowledge
Expert Witnesses
•
O testimony admissible when
(1) Specialized knowledge will help trier of fact understand evidence or determine

fact in issue
(2) Based on sufficient facts or data
(3) Product of reliable principles and methods, and
(4) Reliably applied principles and methods to facts of case

Daubert Standard – Reliable Principles and Methods
•
Reliability of theory or technique determined by
(i)  Whether subjected to peer review and publication
(ii) Whether tested
(iii) Rate of error
(iv) Whether accepted in scientific community
California – Kelly/Frye Standard
•
Required to prove new theory or technique has been accepted in scientific
community
Criminal Cases
•
O re D’s mental state that constitutes element of crime is inadmissible
Present Recollection Refreshed
•
Witness may inspect object to refresh recollection re any matter about which he
testifies
•
Object not admitted or read into evidence

•

•

•

•

•
Inadmissible
Criminal Cases
•
Admissible unless probative value substantially outweighed
by prejudicial effect
•
Cross-examination and extrinsic evidence

       •  Always admissible unless more than 10 years old

       Crimes Not Involving Dishonesty
       •   Admissible unless probative value is substantially outweighed by prejudicial
             effect

              Witness is the Defendant
              •  Must be admitted if probative value substantially outweighs
                     prejudicial effect

              Witness is Not the Defendant
              •   Generally admissible

        California
       •   Admissible only if crime involved moral turpitude

       Convictions More Than Ten Years Old
       •   Admissible if
             (1) Probative value substantially outweighs prejudicial effect, and
             (2) Proponent gives adverse party rx notice

  ✷  Specific Instances of Misconduct
       •   Inadmissible to attack or support witness’s character for truthfulness
       •  May be allowed on cross-examination

      ✷  California

          ✷  Civil Cases
                     •   Inadmissible

          ✷  Criminal Cases
                     •   Admissible unless probative value substantially outweighed
                       by prejudicial effect
                     •   Cross-examination and extrinsic evidence

Reputation or Opinion
•
May be used to attack witness’s credibility re character for truthfulness
•
Evidence of truthfulness admissible only after character attacked
Sensory Deficiencies
•
Establishing mental or physical capabilities were impaired
•
Cross-examination and extrinsic evidence

Rehabilitating an Impeached Witness
(i) Redirect examination
(ii) Another witness’s testimony re impeached witness’s truthfulness through O, R, or
(iii) Introducing prior consistent statement
HEARSAY

Hearsay
•
Out-of-court statement offered to prove TOMA
Hearsay Within Hearsay
•
Not excluded if each part conforms with exception

Legally Operative Facts
•
Non-hearsay if offered to prove statement was made
Effect on Listener
•
Non-hearsay if offered to show effect on person who heard statement
Declarant’s State of Mind
•
Non-hearsay if offered as circumstantial evidence of declarant’s state of mind
Non-Human Declarations
•
Non-hearsay if asserted by non-human
HEARSAY EXEMPTIONS

Prior Inconsistent Statements
•
Admissible as substantive evidence and for impeachment

California
•
Admissible as substantive evidence and for impeachment
Prior Consistent Statements
•
Not hearsay if offered to
(i)  Rebut charge of recent fabrication or improper motive, or
(ii) Rehabilitate declarant’s credibility

California
•
Not hearsay if offered to
(i)  Rebut charge of recent fabrication or improper motive, or
(ii) Rehabilitate declarant’s credibility
Prior Statements of Identification
•
Admissible if identifying declarant testifies and is subject to crossexamination

California
•
Admissible if
(i)  Identifying declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination
(ii) Identification is of person who participated in crime
(iii) Statement made when crime was fresh in declarant’s memory, or
(iv) Declarant testifies identification was true reflection
Party Opponent Statements
•
Not hearsay if made by party to litigation and offered by opposing party
Adoptive Admissions
•
Offered by opposing party against party who adopted statement

Adoption by Silence
•
Rx person under same circumstances would have denied statement if not true
Vicarious Admissions
•
Offered by opposing party made by
(i)  Person whom party authorized to make statement
(ii) Party’s agent or employee within scope of relationship, or
(iii) Co-conspirator during and in furtherance of conspiracy

Unavailable Declarant Requirement
(i) Court privilege applies
(ii) Refusal to testify despite court order
(iii) Testimony of not remembering subject
(iv) Death or illness, or
(v) Absence and proponent unable to obtain attendance or testimony

Former Testimony
•
Admissible if party against whom offered had opportunity and similar motive
to develop testimony

(3) Accurately reflects knowledge
•
May be read into evidence if admitted
•
May be admitted as exhibit only if offered by adverse party
Business Records
(1) Made at or near time by someone with knowledge
(2) Kept in course of regularly conducted business
(3) Making record was regular practice, and
(4) Source or method was trustworthy
Public Records
(1) Sets out

(i) Office’s activities
(ii) Matter observed while under legal duty to report
(iii) Factual findings from legally authorized investigation, and
(2) Source or method was trustworthy

Learned Treatises
(1) Statement called to attention of expert witness on cross-examination or relied on

during direct examination, and
(2) Publication is established as reliable authority
•
May be read into evidence but not admitted as exhibit

Residual Catch-All
•
If no other exception applies, still admitted if
(1) Supported by sufficient guarantees of trustworthiness
(2) Offered as evidence of material fact, and
(3) More probative on point for which it’s offered than any other evidence that

proponent can obtain through rx efforts
PRIVILEGES

Lawyer-Client Privilege
•
Communications between lawyer and client related to representation are protected
•
Lasts indefinitely

California
•
Communications between lawyer and client related to representation
 are protected
•
Ends when client’s estate fully distributed and representative discharged

Exceptions
(i) Client’s objective to engage in future crime or fraud
(ii) Communication relevant in malpractice action against lawyer, or
(iii) Civil litigation between formerly joint clients of lawyer

blocks.text:
California
(i) Client’s objective to engage in future crime or fraud
(ii) Communication relevant in malpractice action against lawyer
(iii) Civil litigation between formerly joint clients of lawyer, or
(iv) Lawyer rxly believes disclosure necessary to prevent criminal act likely to result in

death or substantial bodily harm
Attorney Work Product Doctrine

Qualified Immunity
•
Docs prepared in anticipation of litigation not discoverable unless
(1) Substantial need, and
(2) Undue hardship
Absolute Immunity
•
Lawyer’s mental impressions, research, opinions, etc. not discoverable unless
immunity waived
California – Physician-Patient Privilege
•
Communications between licensed physician and patient for purpose of diagnosis or
treatment are protected

Exceptions
(i) Patient puts condition into issue
(ii) Communication for illegal purpose
(iii) Dispute between physician and patient, or
(iv) Privilege is waived

Psychotherapist-Patient or Social-Worker Patient Privilege
•
Communications between psychotherapist or social worker and client are protected

Exceptions
(i) Patient puts condition into issue
(ii) Communication for illegal purpose
(iii)  Dispute between physician and patient, or
(iv) Patient is danger to himself or others

Spousal Immunity
•
Criminal cases
•
Spouse of criminal D may not be called to testify against D
•
Spouses may not be compelled to testify against each other
•
Only applicable while in valid marriage
•
Covers testimony re matters prior to and during marriage
Confidential Marital Communications
•
Communications between spouses during marriage are protected
•
Survives after marriage is over

blocks.text:
Subsequent Remedial Measures
•
Inadmissible to prove
(i) Negligence
(ii) Culpable conduct
(iii) Need for warning or instruction, or
(iv) Defect in product or design
•
Admissible to prove
(i)  Ownership
(ii) Control
(iii) Feasibility of precautionary measures, or
(iv) For impeachment
Liability Insurance
•
Inadmissible to prove whether person acted negligently or wrongfully
•
Admissible to prove
(i)  Bias or prejudice
(ii) Agency
(iii) Ownership, or
(iv) Control
Compromise Offers and Negotiations
•
Inadmissible to prove or disprove validity or amount of disputed claim
•
Admissible to
(i) Prove bias or prejudice
(ii) Negate contention of undue delay, or
(iii) Prove effort to obstruct criminal investigation or prosecution
Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
•
Inadmissible to prove liability for injury

California
•
Inadmissible to prove liability for injury
Pleas and Related Statements
•
Inadmissible to prove D’s guilt
California – Expressions of Sympathy
•
Inadmissible as evidence of admission in civil actions
•
Statements of fault accompanying expression of sympathy admissible
CONFRONTATION CLAUSE

Confrontation Clause
•
D has right to confront all witnesses testifying against him

  ✷  Subsequent Remedial Measures
       •   Inadmissible to prove
                (i)  Negligence
                (ii) Culpable conduct
                (iii) Need for warning or instruction, or
              (iv) Defect in product or design
       •   Admissible to prove
                (i) Ownership
                (ii) Control
                (iii) Feasibility of precautionary measures, or
              (iv) For impeachment

  ✷  Liability Insurance
       •   Inadmissible to prove whether person acted negligently or wrongfully
       •   Admissible to prove
                (i) Bias or prejudice
                (ii) Agency
                (iii) Ownership, or
              (iv) Control

  ✷  Compromise Offers and Negotiations
       •   Inadmissible to prove or disprove validity or amount of disputed claim
       •   Admissible to
                (i) Prove bias or prejudice
                (ii) Negate contention of undue delay, or
                (iii) Prove effort to obstruct criminal investigation or prosecution

  ✷  Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
       •   Inadmissible to prove liability for injury

      ✷  California
              •   Inadmissible to prove liability for injury

        Pleas and Related Statements
       •   Inadmissible to prove D’s guilt

        California – Expressions of Sympathy
       •   Inadmissible as evidence of admission in civil actions
       •   Statements of fault accompanying expression of sympathy admissible

CONFRONTATION CLAUSE

  ✷  Confrontation Clause
       •  D has right to confront all witnesses testifying against him



                                          12                                          Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

(1) Testimonial
(2) Declarant unavailable, and

blocks.text:
PARTNERSHIPS
FORMATION AND MANAGEMENT
Partnership Formation
•
Association of 2+ persons to carry on for-profit business as co-owners who share profits
Agreement
•
Agreement required
•
No writing required unless SOF
Sharing of Profits
•
Partnership presumed if profits shared
Intent
•
Must intend to do business as co-owners
Profits and Losses
•
Each partner receives equal profits and losses unless agreement states otherwise
Management Rights and Termination of Authority
•
Each partner has equal management rights unless agreement states otherwise
•
Matters within ordinary course of business: Majority
•
Matters outside course of business: Unanimous consent
RELATIONSHIPS WITH THIRD PARTIES
Power to Bind Partnership
•
Partner with actual or apparent authority may bind partnership to K
Express Actual Authority
•
Created through
•
Agreement
•
Statement of authority, or
•
Authorization
Implied Actual Authority
•
Look to partner’s rx understanding of partnership’s objective
Apparent Actual Authority
•
3P rxly believes partner has authority
Partnership’s Liability for Partner’s Torts
•
Partnership liable for torts committed by partner within ordinary course of business or with
partnership authority
Liability to Third Parties
•
Partnership can sue or be sued
•
All partners jointly and severally liable

blocks.text:
Incoming Partners
•
Not liable for partnership obligation incurred before admission
Outgoing Partners
•
Liable for partnership obligation incurred while still partner
•
Release, payment, or novation absolves liability
Indemnification
•
May seek indemnification for excess payment
Effect of Judgment
•
Creditor must deplete partnership’s assets before seizing individual partner’s assets
FIDUCIARY DUTIES
Duty of Loyalty
•
Partner must not
•
Compete with partnership
•
Advance interest adverse to partnership, or
•
Engage in self-dealing
Usurping partnership Opportunity
•
Partners must disclose all partnership opportunities
•
May only personally pursue opportunity after rejected by co-partners
Duty of Care
•
Partner must not engage in
•
Grossly negligent or reckless conduct
•
Intentional misconduct, or
•
Knowing violations of the law
PARTNERSHIP CHANGES AND TERMINATION
Dissociation
•
Partner ceases to be associated
•
Voluntary or involuntary
Dissolution
At Will
•
No fixed term or undertaking
•
Dissolves when partner dissociates by giving notice
For a Term or Undertaking
•
Dissolves when term expires or partners agree
Winding Up
•
After dissolution, partnership exists only to wind up

blocks.text:
Order of Distribution of Assets
(1) Pay off creditors
(2) Repay partners
(3) Allocate profits
OTHER PARTNERSHIP FORMS
Limited Partnership (LP)
•
Formed by 2+ persons
•
Requires at least 1 general partner and at least 1 limited partner
Liability of Partners
Limited Partners
•
Liable if participates in control of business or is general partner
General Partners
•
Liable to 3Ps
Failure to File
•
If LP not validly formed, person who, in good faith, believes she is limited partner
may be liable to 3Ps
Limited Liability Partnership (LLP)
•
Partners not liable for partnership obligations but liable for own misconduct

Duty to Accept
•

Duty to Reject
•

(ii) L’s physical or mental condition materially impairs ability to represent

Client
•
Determines objectives of representation
Lawyer

•

(i)  Representation will lead to violation of ethics or law
(ii) L’s mental or physical condition materially impairs ability to represent, or
(iii) L is discharged

California

•

(iii) C breaches material term relating to representation and L has given rx warning
(iv) Representation will result in unrx financial burden on L
(v) C makes representation unrxly difficult, or
(vi) Other good cause

blocks.text:
•
Factors
(i)  Whether L engaged in fraud
(ii) Whether L failed to disclose material facts
(iii) Amount of fee in proportion to value of services
(iv) Relative sophistication of L and C
(v) Difficulty of case
(vi) L’s experience and reputation
(vii) Fee customarily charged in locality for similar services
(viii) Preclusion of other employment
(ix) Whether fixed or contingent
(x) Nature of relationship with C
(xi) Serviced performed and results obtained
(xii) Whether C gave informed consent
General Fee Agreements
•
Need not be in writing

California
•
General fee agreements > $1,000 must be in writing and signed by both parties unless
(i) C is corporation
(ii) C provides written waiver
(iii) Services provided in emergency to prevent fx prejudice to C, or
(iv) Fee is implied based on previous similar services provided by L

Contents of Fee Agreement
•
Fee agreement must include
(1) Basis of compensation
(2) General nature of legal services provided, and
(3) Responsibilities of L and C
Contingent Fees
(1) Writing signed by C
(2) Method of determination, and
(3) Clearly notify C of fees

California
(1) Writing signed by both C and L
(2) Method of determination
(3) Explain how disbursements and costs will affect fee and C’s recovery
(4) State fee is negotiable, and
(5) Clearly notify C of fees
Limitation on Contingent Fees
•
Prohibited in criminal cases and domestic relations matters

blocks.text:
California
•
Allowed in domestic relations matters if not contingent upon securing
divorce or amount of spousal or child support

Lawyers at Same Firm
•
Ls at same firm may share fees
Lawyers at Different Firms
•
Ls at different firms cannot split fees unless
(1) C consents to division in writing
(2) Total fee is rx, and
(3) Division proportionate to services by each L or each L jointly responsible

California
•
Ls at different firms cannot split fees unless
(1) Ls enter into written agreement
(2) C consents in writing at time of agreement or after full written disclosure,

and
(3) Total fee not increased solely because of agreement to share
Non-Lawyers
•
Ls prohibited from
(i) Sharing legal fees with non-L, or
(ii) Forming partnership with non-L if any partnership activities involve practice of

law
•
Exceptions
(i)  Agreement to distribute fees to L’s estate after L’s death
(ii) Agreed-upon purchase price from practice of deceased or disabled L
(iii) Fees to non-L employees through compensation or retirement plan, or
(iv) Court-awarded legal fees shared with nonprofit organization that employed,

retained, recommended L
Referral Fees
•
L prohibited from compensating or giving anything of value for recommending L’s
services

California
•
L allowed to give gift or gratuity for recommending L’s services as long as
not offered in consideration of agreement that
(i)  Referral would be expected, or
(ii) Person would make future referrals

blocks.text:
Reciprocal Referral Agreements
•
Allowed if
(1) Not exclusive, and
(2) C aware of existence and nature of agreement
CLIENT’S FUND ACCOUNTS AND PROPERTY

Duty to Safeguard Client’s Money
•
L may not commingle funds
•
L must put any money connected with C’s representation in separate trust account
Dispute Over Funds
•
Disputed portion shall be safeguarded and kept separately from that of L
Duty to Safeguard Client’s Property
•
C’s property shall be safeguarded and kept separately from that of L
Recordkeeping
•
Complete records of account funds shall be
(1) Kept by L, and
(2) Preserved for 5 years after termination of representation
PRIVILEGE AND CONFIDENTIALITY

Lawyer-Client Privilege
•
Communications between L and C re representation protected if
(1) C intended for communication to be confidential, and
(2) Communication is for purpose of obtaining legal advice
•
Waivable only by C
•
Lasts indefinitely

California
•
Communications between L and C re representation protected if
(1) C intended for communication to be confidential, and
(2) Communication is for purpose of obtaining legal advice
•
Waivable only by C
•
Ends when C’s estate fully distributed and representative discharged
Model Rule Exception
•
L-C privilege does not apply if
(i) C’s objective is to engage in future crime or fraud
(ii) Communication relevant to issue in malpractice action against L, or
(iii) Civil litigation between formerly joint Cs of L

blocks.text:
California
•
L-C privilege does not apply if
(i)  C’s objective is to engage in future crime or fraud
(ii) Communication relevant to issue in malpractice action against L
(iii) Civil litigation between formerly joint Cs of L, or
(iv) L rxly believes disclosure necessary to prevent criminal act likely to

result in death or substantial bodily harm
Work Product

Qualified Immunity
•
Docs prepared in anticipation of litigation not discoverable unless
(1) Substantial need, and
(2) Undue hardship
Absolute Immunity
•
L’s mental impressions, research, opinions, etc. not discoverable unless immunity
waived

Duty of Confidentiality
•
L must not reveal info about representation unless
(i)  C gives informed consent, or
(ii) Disclosure impliedly authorized to fulfill representation
•
L may reveal info if
(i)  Disclosure required by law or court order
(ii) Prevent rxly certain death or substantial bodily harm
(iii) Prevent C from committing crime rxly certain to result in substantial injury to another’s

financial or property interests
(iv) Secure legal advice about L’s compliance with rules of ethics, or
(v) Establish claim or defense in action between L and C

California
•
L must not reveal info about representation without C’s informed consent
•
L may disclose C’s confidential info if rxly believes it will prevent criminal act likely
to result in death or substantial bodily harm
•
Before disclosure, L must
(1) Make good faith effort to persuade C not to commit crime, and
(2) Inform C of L’s ability or decision to disclose
COMMUNICATION WITH CLIENT

Duty to Communicate
•
L must
(i)  Keep C rxly informed re status of matter
(ii) Provide prompt responses to C’s requests for info
(iii) Promptly inform C re situations where C’s consent is necessary

blocks.text:
(iv) Consult with C re relevant limitation on L’s conduct
COMPETENCE & DILIGENCE

Duty of Competence
•
L must provide competent representation to C

California
•
L shall not intentionally, recklessly, or repeatedly fail to perform with competence

Exception
•
In emergency, L allowed to provide assistance even if lacking requisite competence if
consulting with another L impractical
Lack of Competence
•
L lacking competence must
(i)  Become competent through preparation and study
(ii) Associate with rxly competent L
(iii) Decline representation, or
(iv) Withdraw
Duty of Diligence
•
L must act with rx diligence and promptness

California
•
L shall not intentionally or recklessly fail to act with rx diligence
DUTY OF LOYALTY

Duty of Loyalty
•
L must act in best interest of C
CONFLICTING INTERESTS

Current Clients
•
L must not represent C whose interests are directly adverse to another C or if significant risk
that representation will be materially limited by L’s responsibilities to another C, former C, or
3P unless
(1) L rxly believes he will be able to provide competent and diligent representation
(2) Representation not prohibited by law
(3) L not representing adversaries of same litigation, and
(4) Each C gives informed consent, confirmed in writing

California
•

another C, former C, or 3P unless
(1) L rxly believes he will be able to provide competent and diligent representation
(2) Representation not prohibited by law
(3) L not representing adversaries of same litigation, and
(4) Each C gives informed written consent
Aggregate Settlements Between Co-Clients

Civil Cases
•
L representing multiple parties required to get informed written consent from each C
Criminal Cases
•
L representing multiple parties required to get informed written consent from each C
Former Clients

Former Clients of Lawyer
•
L must not represent new C in same or substantially related matter in which he
represented former C when new C’s interests are materially adverse
•
Unless former C gives informed consent, confirmed in writing
Former Clients of Lawyer’s Law Firm
•
L must not
(i)  Represent C whose interests materially adverse to former C previously

represented by L’s law firm in same or substantially related matter, or
(ii) Use or reveal info re representation of former C’s disadvantage
•
Unless former C gives informed consent, confirmed in writing
Former Government Employee
•
L who formerly served as public officer or government employee must not represent
C in connection with matter in which L participated
•
Unless government agency gives informed consent, confirmed in writing
Prospective Client
•
P must not represent C with interests materially adverse to those of prospective C in same or
substantially related matter if L received info from prospective C that could be significantly
harmful unless
(i)  Both C and prospective C give informed consent, confirmed in writing, or
(ii) L who received info took rx measures to avoid exposure to more info, was timely

screened, received no share of fee, and promptly gave written notice to prospective C
Organizational Client
•
L must act in best interests of organization

blocks.text:
Dual Representation
•
L can concurrently represent organization and its constituents if
(1) Conflict of interest rules satisfied, and
(2) L gets consent by organization official other than person to be individually

represented
Reporting Misconduct
•
L must report action of constituents to higher authority if L knows actions
(1) Likely to cause substantial harm to organization, and
(2) Violate law or legal obligation
•
Unless L rxly believes not in best interest of organization to do so

Higher Authority’s Failure to Act
•
L may report outside organization if action
(1) Clearly violation of law, and
(2) L rxly believes violation will cause substantial injury to organization

California
•
L must not report violation outside organization
•
L may try to convince highest authority to reconsider or resign
CONFLICTED INTERESTS CONTINUED – VARIOUS ISSUES

Business Transactions with Clients
(1) Terms are fair and fully disclosed in writing in manner C can rxly understand
(2) C is advised in writing to consult independent counsel and is given rx opportunity to do so,

and
(3) C gives informed consent, in writing signed by C
Sexual Relations
•
L must not have sexual relations with C unless consensual sexual relationship existed prior to
L-C relationship
Proprietary Interest in Cause of Action
•
L must not obtain proprietary interest in COA
•
L may
(i)  Obtain lien to secure L’s fees or expenses, or
(ii) Contract with C for rx contingency fee in civil case
Literary or Media Rights
•
L must not make agreement giving L literary or media rights to account based on info relating
to representation before conclusion of representation

                     Higher Authority’s Failure to Act
                     •  L may report outside organization if action
                             (1) Clearly violation of law, and
                             (2) L rxly believes violation will cause substantial injury to organization

                                California
                            •  L must not report violation outside organization
                            •  L may try to convince highest authority to reconsider or resign

CONFLICTED INTERESTS CONTINUED – VARIOUS ISSUES

  ✷  Business Transactions with Clients
        (1) Terms are fair and fully disclosed in writing in manner C can rxly understand
        (2) C is advised in writing to consult independent counsel and is given rx opportunity to do so,
         and
        (3) C gives informed consent, in writing signed by C

  ✷  Sexual Relations
       •  L must not have sexual relations with C unless consensual sexual relationship existed prior to
         L-C relationship

  ✷  Proprietary Interest in Cause of Action
       •  L must not obtain proprietary interest in COA
       •  L may
                (i) Obtain lien to secure L’s fees or expenses, or
                (ii) Contract with C for rx contingency fee in civil case

        Literary or Media Rights
       •  L must not make agreement giving L literary or media rights to account based on info relating
             to representation before conclusion of representation

                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
Solicited Gifts
•
L must not solicit substantial gift from C or prepare instrument giving substantial gift to L or
L’s relative unless L or recipient is related to C

California
•
L must not solicit substantial gift from C or prepare instrument giving substantial gift
to L or L’s relative unless
(i) L or recipient is related to C, or
(ii) C is advised by independent counsel
Providing Financial Assistance
•
L must not provide financial assistance to C unless
(i)  L is advancing court costs and litigation expenses
(ii) L is advancing court costs and litigation expenses for indigent C, or
(iii) L is representing indigent C pro bono

California
•
L must not provide financial assistance to C unless
(i)  L is paying such expenses to 3P from funds collected for C as result of

representation
(ii) L agrees to lend money to C based on C’s written promise to repay loan
(iii)L is advancing court costs and litigation expenses, or
(iv)L is advancing court costs and litigation expenses for indigent C
Third-Party Compensation to Lawyer
•
L must not accept 3P compensation on behalf of C unless
(1) C gives informed consent
(2) No interference with L’s professional judgment, and
(3) Info re representation of C is protected

California
•
L must not accept 3P compensation on behalf of C unless
(1) C gives informed written consent
(2) No interference with L’s professional judgment, and
(3) Info re representation of C is protected
Malpractice Liability

Prospective Limitation on Liability
•
L may not contract to prospectively limit unless C is independently represented

California
•
L must not contract with client to prospectively limit

blocks.text:
Settling a Malpractice Claim
•
L may not settle claim unless
(1) C advised in writing to seek advice of independent counsel, and
(2) C is given rx opportunity to do so
Opposing Lawyers Related
•
Related Ls may not represent opposing parties in same manner unless each C provides
informed consent

California
•
L cannot represent C without providing C written disclosure if L knows that another
party in matter
(i)  Is related to L
(ii) Lives with L
(iii) Is C of L or another L in his firm, or
(iv) Has intimate personal relationship with L
Lawyer as Witness
•
L cannot represent C in manner in which L likely to be necessary witness unless
(i)  Testimony related to uncontested issue
(ii) Testimony relates to nature or value of legal services rendered, or
(iii) Disqualification of L would result in substantial hardship to L

California
•
L cannot represent C in manner in which L likely to be necessary witness unless
(i)  Testimony related to uncontested issue
(ii) Testimony relates to nature or value of legal services rendered, or
(iii) C provides informed written consent
Imputed Disqualification
•
Conflict of interest as to L imputed onto entire firm unless
(i)  Conflict is personal in nature and does not present significant risk of representation

materially limited, or
(ii) Conflict relates to former C of L’s prior firm, L is timely screened, written notice

provided to former C
•
C may waive with informed consent, confirmed in writing

California
•
Conflict of interest as to L imputed onto entire firm unless
(i)  Conflict is personal in nature and does not present significant risk of

representation materially limited,
(ii) L did not substantially participate
(iii)Conflict relates to former C of L’s prior firm, L is timely screened, written notice

provided to former C, or
•
C may waive with informed written consent

blocks.text:
Duty of Candor to the Court
•
L must not
(i)  Make or fail to correct false statement of law or fact
(ii) Fail to disclose legal authority in controlling jx that is adverse to C’s position, or
(iii) Offer false evidence
Duty to Avoid Frivolous Claims
•
L must not file frivolous claims and defenses

Duty to Expedite Litigation
•
L must make rx efforts to expedite litigation
Duty of Fairness to Opposing Party or Counsel

Discovery
•
L must not make frivolous discovery requests or fail to rxly comply with
legally proper discovery requests
Evidence

Access to Evidence
•
L must not unlawfully obstruct access to evidence or alter, destroy,
or conceal docs and other evidence
False Evidence
•
L must not
(i)  Falsify evidence
(ii) Counsel or assist witness to give false testimony, or
(iii)Unlawfully offer inducement to witness

Reasonable Belief
•
If L, C, or witness has offered evidence L comes to know is
false, L must take rx remedial measures

Criminal Defendants
•
L cannot prohibit criminal D from testifying on own
behalf
•
If D will testify falsely, L must attempt to dissuade
C from giving false testimony → Attempt to
withdraw → Make disclosure to court

blocks.text:
California
•
If C will testify falsely, L must attempt to
dissuade C from giving false testimony →
Attempt to withdraw → Offer testimony in
narrative form
Learning of Falsity After the Fact
•
If L learns of false testimony after the fact, L must attempt to
dissuade C from giving false testimony or convince C to
correct → Attempt to withdraw → Make disclosure to court
as rxly necessary

California
•
If L learns of false testimony after the fact, L must
attempt to dissuade C from giving false testimony or
convince C to correct → Attempt to withdraw →
Make disclosure to court but no confidential C info
Ex Parte Communications
•
L must not seek to influence or communicate ex parte with jurors or judge
Payments to Witnesses

Lay Witnesses
•
L may pay for rx costs incurred
Expert Witnesses
•
L may pay according to fee schedule and other rx costs
•
L may not provide contingency fee
Communications with Represented Parties
•
L must not communicate with persons L knows are represented by another L
unless
(i) Party’s L has consented, or
(ii) Communication authorized by law or court order
Trial Publicity
•
L participating in investigation or litigation must not make out-of-court
statement he knows will be publicly distributed and have substantial
likelihood of material prejudice

Exceptions
•
Making statements to protect C from prejudice
•
Stating claim, offense, defense, persons involved
•
Sharing info in public record
•
Stating investigation in progress

blocks.text:
•
Requesting assistance in obtaining evidence
•
Statements re those involved in criminal case
California – Threats
•
L cannot attempt to obtain advantage in civil case by threatening charges
Special Responsibilities of Prosecutors
•
Prosecutors must
(i)  Refrain from proceeding with case unsupported by probable cause
(ii)  Make rx efforts to assure that accused has been advised to obtain counsel and

given opportunity to do so
(iii) Not pursue waiver of important pretrial rights from unrepresented accused
(iv) Make timely disclosure of exculpatory evidence, and
(v) Refrain from making out-of-court comments substantially likely to heighten

public condemnation of accused
PROFESSIONAL INTEGRITY CONTINUED

Non-Lawyers Within Firm
•
L having direct supervisory authority over non-L responsible for ethics violations if
(i)  L ordered or ratified conduct, or
(ii) L is partner at firm or directly supervised non-L and could have mitigated

consequences
California – Employment of Disbarred, Suspended, Resigned, or Involuntarily Inactive Lawyer
•
L must not employ, associate with, or assist person L knows is member who is disbarred,
suspended, resigned, or involuntarily inactive

California – Exceptions
•
L may employ, associate with, or assist ineligible person to perform research,
drafting or clerical activities
•
L must serve written notice to State Bar
Professional Misconduct of Lawyer
•
Violate ethics rules
•
Commit crime that reflects adversely on L’s honesty, trustworthiness, fitness
•
Engage in fraudulent conduct
•
Engage in conduct prejudicial to administration of justice
•
State or imply ability to improperly influence government agency or official
•
Knowingly assist judge or judicial officer in violation of ethics or law
•
Engage in harassment or discrimination

blocks.text:
Reporting Professional Misconduct
•
L must report to appropriate authority when L knows another L or judge has violated ethics in
manner that raises substantial question re honesty, trustworthiness, fitness
Unauthorized Practice of Law
•
L must not practice law in state where not licensed or assist another in doing so
•
L may be permitted to temporarily practice in jx if
(i)  Pro hac vice, or
(ii) In association with licensed L
ADVERTISING AND SOLICITATION

Truth in Communication
•
L must not make false or materially misleading communication re himself or services
Contents of Ad
•
L can advertise services if
(1) Ad provides name and contact info for at least 1 L or firm responsible for content, and
(2) If L states or implies certification, L has indeed been certified and identifies certifying

Direct Solicitation Prohibited
•
L must not solicit prospective Cs through live person-to-person contact unless
prospective C is
(i)  L
(ii) Person with familial, close, professional relationship with L or firm, or
(iii) Person routinely using type of services offered by L for business purposes
Targeted Mailings, Recordings, or Ads
•
Written, recorded, electronic communications must include “Advertisement” on
outside envelope and at beginning and ending of communication unless ad apparent
from context
Limitation on Soliciting Prospective Clients
•
L must not solicit employment if
(i)  Prospective C has communicated desire not to be solicited, or
(ii) Involves coercion, duress, harassment

       •  L must report to appropriate authority when L knows another L or judge has violated ethics in
          manner that raises substantial question re honesty, trustworthiness, fitness

  ✷  Unauthorized Practice of Law
       •  L must not practice law in state where not licensed or assist another in doing so
       •  L may be permitted to temporarily practice in jx if
                (i) Pro hac vice, or
                (ii) In association with licensed L

ADVERTISING AND SOLICITATION

  ✷  Truth in Communication
       •  L must not make false or materially misleading communication re himself or services

  ✷  Contents of Ad
       •  L can advertise services if
             (1) Ad provides name and contact info for at least 1 L or firm responsible for content, and
             (2) If L states or implies certification, L has indeed been certified and identifies certifying
                organization

         Solicitation of Clients

      ✷  Direct Solicitation Prohibited
              •  L must not solicit prospective Cs through live person-to-person contact unless
                   prospective C is
                          (i) L
                           (ii) Person with familial, close, professional relationship with L or firm, or
                           (iii) Person routinely using type of services offered by L for business purposes

               Targeted Mailings, Recordings, or Ads
              •   Written, recorded, electronic communications must include “Advertisement” on
                    outside envelope and at beginning and ending of communication unless ad apparent
                 from context

                Limitation on Soliciting Prospective Clients
              •  L must not solicit employment if
                          (i) Prospective C has communicated desire not to be solicited, or
                           (ii) Involves coercion, duress, harassment

                                          15                                          Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                                      sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
REAL PROPERTY
OWNERSHIP
PRESENT ESTATES AND FUTURE INTERESTS
Fee Simple Absolute
Fee Simple Absolute
•
Owner has absolute ownership of both present and future possessory rights
•
Duration: Indefinite and potentially infinite
•
Alienable, devisable, descendible
•
If owner dies intestate
•
With heirs → Heirs inherit
•
Without heirs → Escheat to state
Words of Limitation
•
Common law: Words of limitation required
•
Modern law: Default for conveyances without words of limitation
Defeasible Fees
Defeasible Fee
•
Duration: Potentially infinite
•
Subject to termination by occurrence of specified condition
Fee Simple Determinable
•
Created by durational language
•
Alienable, devisable, descendible
Possibility of Reverter
•
Once stated condition occurs, ownership reverts to grantor or
his successor
Fee Simple Subject to Condition Subsequent
•
Created by conditional language
•
Duration: Limited
•
Alienable during grantor’s life
•
Devisable and descendible upon grantor’s death
Right of Reentry
•
Grantor must specifically retain right
•
May reclaim upon happening of stated condition
Fee Simple Subject to Executory Interest
•
Created by conditional language
•
Duration: Limited
•
Automatically transfers to 3P upon happening of stated condition

REAL PROPERTY

OWNERSHIP

     PRESENT ESTATES AND FUTURE INTERESTS

              Fee Simple Absolute

                    Fee Simple Absolute
                     •  Owner has absolute ownership of both present and future possessory rights
                     •   Duration: Indefinite and potentially infinite
                     •   Alienable, devisable, descendible
                     •    If owner dies intestate
                         •  With heirs → Heirs inherit
                         •   Without heirs → Escheat to state

                  Words of Limitation
                     •  Common law: Words of limitation required
                     •  Modern law: Default for conveyances without words of limitation

               Defeasible Fees

                       Defeasible Fee
                     •   Duration: Potentially infinite
                     •   Subject to termination by occurrence of specified condition

              ✷  Fee Simple Determinable

                            •   Alienable, devisable, descendible

                            •   Devisable and descendible upon grantor’s death

                  ✷  Right of Reentry
                                    •   Grantor must specifically retain right
                                    •  May reclaim upon happening of stated condition

              ✷  Fee Simple Subject to Executory Interest
                            •   Created by conditional language
                            •   Duration: Limited
                            •   Automatically transfers to 3P upon happening of stated condition

blocks.text:
Executory Interests
•
Belong to 3P
•
Transferrable
Life Estate
•
Duration: Measured by a life
Reversion
•
Held by grantor who retains right to reclaim
Remainder
•
Held by 3P and arises upon end of life estate
Restraint on Alienation
•
Generally void
•
May be valid if rx and for limited duration
CONCURRENT ESTATES
Tenancy in Common
•
Held by 2 or more tenants
•
No ROS
•
Each tenant has undivided interest and right to possess whole property
•
Devisable, transferable, descendible
Joint Tenancy
•
Owned by 2 or more tenants
•
ROS
•
Alienable
•
Modern law: Survivorship language and clear intent required
•
Common law: 4 unities required
(1) Time
(2) Title
(3) Interest, and
(4) Possession
Severance
•
Converts severed share into tenancy in common
Conveyance at Death
•
No effect
•
Other joint tenants have ROS
Sale
•
Severs joint tenancy with respect to seller

blocks.text:
Mortgage
Title Theory
•
Severs joint tenancy, resulting in tenancy in common
Lien Theory
•
Severs joint tenancy upon foreclosure sale after default
Judicial Lien
•
No severance unless levy and sale of interest
Rights and Obligations of Tenants
Possession
•
Each tenant has right to possess whole property
Ouster
•
Ousted tenant may sue for access and damages
Adverse Possession
•
Ousted tenant may make claim for adverse possession
•
See Adverse Possession
Rent
•
Co-tenants do not pay other co-tenants for use
•
If renting property to 3P, must share rent minus operating and repair
expenses
Operating Expenses
•
Co-tenant can request contribution if paid more than his share
Repairs and Improvements
•
Co-tenant may not request reimbursement for repairs
Partition
•
Any tenant may unilaterally partition property
LANDLORD AND TENANT
LEASEHOLDS
Tenancy for Years
•
Fixed period
•
Created by express agreement
•
Must satisfy SOF if for 1+ year
Termination
•
Terminates automatically upon end of term
•
No notice required

blocks.text:
Renewal
•
Right to renew must be expressly stated
Periodic Tenancy
•
Repetitive and continuous measured by fixed period
•
No expiration date
•
Created by express agreement, implication, or operation of law
•
SOF not applicable
Termination
•
Notice required at least 1 period in advance
Renewal
•
Automatically renews
Tenancy at Will
•
No specific duration
•
Created by express agreement or implication
Termination
•
May be terminated at any time by either party
Right to Terminate
•
If agreement only gives right to landlord → Same right implied for tenant
•
If agreement only gives right to tenant → Same right not implied for landlord
Tenancy at Sufferance
•
Tenant wrongfully remains in possession after tenancy expires
Termination
•
Terminates when tenant vacates or landlord evicts
TENANT DUTIES
Tenant Duties
Rent
•
Duty to pay rent
•
If failure to perform, landlord may sue for damages and evict
Abandonment
•
Common law: If tenant abandons, landlord may reclaim
Waste
•
Tenant must repair damages for which he is responsible
Affirmative Waste
•
Tenant may not cause voluntary waste

blocks.text:
Ameliorative Waste
•
Tenant may make rxly necessary physical alterations which increase
property value
Permissive Waste
•
Tenant must repair property to preserve original condition
•
No duty to repair normal wear and tear
LANDLORD DUTIES
Landlord Duties
Possession
•
Majority: Duty to deliver actual and legal possession
•
Minority: Duty to deliver legal possession
Repair
•
Residential leases: Duty to repair
•
Commercial leases: No duty to repair
Warranty of Habitability
•
Must maintain property to meet health and safety standards
Remedies
•
If breached, tenant must notify landlord and give rx time for repair
•
If failure to make repairs, tenant may
•
Withhold rent
•
Make repairs and deduct cost from future rent, or
•
Remain in possession and seek damages
Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
•
Tenant entitled to use and enjoyment of property without interference from
landlord
Eviction
Actual Eviction
•
If landlord prevents access to property, lease terminates
Constructive Eviction
•
If landlord creates condition causing substantial interference, tenant
not obligated to pay rent if
(1) Tenant provides notice
(2) Landlord fails to resolve condition, and
(3) Tenant vacates within rx amount of time
Mitigation
•
Landlord has duty to mitigate damages and duty of due diligence to re-let the
property

blocks.text:
ASSIGNMENTS AND SUBLEASES
Assignment
•
Lease may be assigned freely
•
Transfers remainder of tenant’s lease
•
Assignee: Privity of estate with landlord
•
Assignor: Privity of contract with landlord
Rent
•
If assignee does not pay rent, assignor liable
Sublease
•
Lease may be subleased freely
•
Transfer for less than complete duration of tenant’s lease
•
Assignor: Privity of contract and privity of estate with landlord
Rent
•
Assignor liable for rent
Limitations
•
Landlord may only refuse assignment or sublease based on rx grounds pertaining to
property
TITLES
ADVERSE POSSESSION
Adverse Possession
(1) Actual entry
(2) Open and notorious
(3) Hostile
(4) Exclusive, and
(5) Continuous for statutory period
Actual
•
Must be actual entry and possession
Open and Notorious
•
Must occupy in manner that would give owner constructive notice
Hostile
•
Must occupy without owner’s permission
Exclusive
•
Cannot share property with owner but may share with other possessors
Continuous
•
Must be continuous throughout statutory period
•
Tacking allowed to meet statutory period
•
Tacking requires privity between occupants

                     •  Tacking allowed to meet statutory period
                     •  Tacking requires privity between occupants

blocks.text:
TRANSFER BY DEED
Deed
(1) Compliance with formalities
(2) Delivery, and
(3) Acceptance
Formalities
(1) Satisfy SOF
(2) Describe property, and
(3) Identify parties
Statute of Frauds
•
Must be in writing signed by grantor
Exceptions
(i) Part performance
(ii) Promissory estoppel
Intent
•
Delivered when grantor has present intent to transfer
•
Must include words of intent
Transfer to Grantee
•
Creates presumption of intent
Transfer to Third-Party Agents
•
If grantor gives to 3P agent but reverses right to rescind or makes
delivery conditional → No present intent or delivery
Acceptance
•
Generally presumed
Types of Deeds
General Warranty Deed
•
Grantor guarantees against defects created by himself and all prior
titleholders
Present Covenants
Seisin
•
Grantor owns property described in deed
Right to Convey
•
Grantor has right to convey title
Against Encumbrances
•
Property does not contain undisclosed encumbrances

blocks.text:
Future Covenants
Quiet Enjoyment
•
Grantee’s possession and enjoyment will not be disturbed by
3P’s assertion of superior title
Warranty
•
Grantor will defend against 3P’s assertion of title
Further Assurances
•
Grantor will use any means necessary to perfect defective
title
Special Warranty Deed
•
Contains same covenants as general warranty deed
•
Only warrants against defects arising during grantor’s ownership
Quitclaim Deed
•
Guarantees no covenants of title
•
Transfers any interest grantor had
Recording Statutes
•
Unless recording statute governs, common law approach “first in time, first in right”
is default
Types
Notice
•
BFP granted title prior to grantee who failed to record
Bona Fide Purchaser
•
Acquired property for value without notice of prior interests
Race
•
First to record granted title
Race-Notice
•
BFP granted title if (1) records first and (2) acquires without notice
of prior interests
Notice
Actual
•
Grantee with actual notice not granted title in notice or race-notice
jxs
Inquiry
•
Rx inspection of property would reveal conflicting interests

blocks.text:
Constructive
•
All prior interests were recorded
Shelter Rule
•
BFP who acquired title through recording statute’s protection shelters his
grantee
LAND USE
EASEMENTS
Easements
•
Non-possessory interest
•
Landowner gives right to use his land for particular purpose
Types of Easements
Easements Appurtenant
•
Attach to land
•
Subsequent landowners with notice may benefit
Easements in Gross
•
Attach to and benefit specific owner
•
Do not transfer to subsequent landowners
Creation of Easements
Express
Grant
(1) In writing and satisfy SOF
(2) Signed by grantor
(3) Identify parties and property, and
(4) Demonstrate grantor’s intent
Reservation
•
Servient estate holder gives title to property but reserves himself
right to use land for specific purpose
Implied
Necessity
(1) Original piece of land is subdivided, and
(2) No access to easement because subdivision is landlocked
Implication
(1) Common owner’s
(2) Pre-existing use that is
(3) Open and continuous after subdivision, and
(4) Rxly necessary to dominant estate’s use and enjoyment

✷  Constructive
                            •   All prior interests were recorded

          ✷  Shelter Rule
                     •  BFP who acquired title through recording statute’s protection shelters his
                           grantee

LAND USE

     EASEMENTS

              Easements
              •   Non-possessory interest
              •  Landowner gives right to use his land for particular purpose

             Types of Easements

                     Easements Appurtenant
                     •   Attach to land
                     •   Subsequent landowners with notice may benefit

                     Easements in Gross
                     •   Attach to and benefit specific owner
                     •  Do not transfer to subsequent landowners

               Creation of Easements

                      Express

              ✷  Grant
                                 (1) In writing and satisfy SOF
                                 (2) Signed by grantor
                                 (3) Identify parties and property, and
                                 (4) Demonstrate grantor’s intent

                              Reservation
                            •   Servient estate holder gives title to property but reserves himself
                                       right to use land for specific purpose

                      Implied

              ✷  Necessity
                                 (1) Original piece of land is subdivided, and
                                 (2) No access to easement because subdivision is landlocked

              ✷  Implication
                                 (1) Common owner’s
                                 (2) Pre-existing use that is
                                 (3) Open and continuous after subdivision, and
                                 (4) Rxly necessary to dominant estate’s use and enjoyment

blocks.text:
Prescription
•
Continuous, actual, open and notorious, hostile use for statutory period
Estoppel
•
Rx and detrimental reliance of servient estate holder’s permission
Negative
•
Restricts use of land
•
Must be created in writing signed by grantor
Termination
•
Common law: No termination unless stated by grantor
•
Modern law: Several termination methods
Release
•
Express release in writing which satisfies SOF
Merger
•
Dominant estate acquires title to servient estate or vice versa
Severance
•
Holder of easement appurtenant attempts to convey or transfer right
Abandonment
•
Affirmative acts demonstrate intent to abandon
Licenses
•
Grants non-possessory right to enter land for specific purpose
•
Freely revocable unless estoppel
Creation
•
Need not satisfy SOF
•
May be granted orally
COVENANTS
Real Covenants
•
Written promise pertaining to land use
•
Affirmative or negative
•
Burden and benefit
•
Run with the land if
(1) Writing
(2) Intent
(3) Touch and concern
(4) Notice, and
(5) Privity
Writing
•
Must be in writing and satisfy SOF

blocks.text:
Intent
•
Parties must demonstrate intent for covenant to run
Touch and Concern
•
Covenant directly related to use of land
Negative Covenants
•
Run with land if restrict land use
Affirmative Covenants
•
Run with land if require certain land use
Notice
•
Only required for burden to run
Privity
Horizontal Privity
•
Only required for burden to run
•
Parties shared some interest in land at time of agreement
Vertical Privity
•
Required for both benefit and burden to run
•
Relationship between original party and subsequent owner
Burden
•
Subsequent owner must hold estate of same duration as
original party
Benefit
•
Subsequent owner must hold estate that is part of original
party’s interest
Termination
(i) Release
(ii) Merger
(iii) Abandonment
(iv) Estoppel
Equitable Servitudes
•
Covenant enforced through equitable remedy
(1) Writing that satisfies SOF
(2) Intent for servitude to be enforced against subsequent landowners
(3) Touch and concern the land, and
(4) Subsequent purchaser had notice

blocks.text:
Implied Reciprocal Servitudes
(1) Land developer intends to subject all plots of subdivision to
(2) Reciprocal and
(3) Negative covenant
(4) Subsequent landowners have notice, and
(5) Common scheme
Defenses to Enforcement
Unclean Hands
•
P has engaged in unethical, illegal, or improper conduct
Estoppel
•
P makes representation that he will not assert claim against D, and D rxly
relies
Laches
(1)  P unrxly delayed filing COA, and
(2) Delay causes prejudice to D
MORTGAGES AND SECURITY INTERESTS
Mortgage
•
Property interest that acts as security for obligation
•
Must satisfy SOF
•
If borrower defaults, lender may claim land to be sold at public auction
Deed of Trust
•
Mortgage alternative
•
If borrower defaults, lender may privately sell
Purchase Money Mortgage
•
Used by borrower to purchase property
Foreclosure
•
If borrower fails to satisfy obligation, lender may foreclose
•
If multiple interests, terminates all junior interests
GOVERNMENT REGULATION AND ZONING
Zoning
•
Government regulations to control land use relating to general welfare
Void Regulation
•
Must pass rational basis test
Existing Non-Conforming Property
•
Non-conforming uses that pre-exist ordinance may continue unless harmful in nature

blocks.text:
Post-Ordinance Non-Conforming Property
•
Owner intends to violate zoning ordinance through non-conforming use
•
Must show
(1) Complying with ordinance would cause hardship through no fault of his own, and
(2) Variance would not cause public harm
Area Variance
•
Permits development in manner not otherwise permitted due to dimensions or
physical restrictions
Use Variance
•
Permits use in manner not otherwise permitted
THE LAND SALE CONTRACT
Land Sale Contract
•
Must satisfy SOF
•
Once K is executed, deed merges with K
Marketable Title
•
Seller has duty to convey marketable title
•
Free from rx doubt of litigation
NUISANCE
Private Nuisance
•
Substantial and unrx interference with another’s use and enjoyment of his land
Substantial Interference
•
Offensive to average person in community
Unreasonable Interference
•
P’s injury outweighs utility of D’s actions
Public Nuisance
•
Unrx interference with rights of public
•
P suffers harm unique to that suffered by public
