blocks.text:
Nominal Damages
•
Elements of COA established but no substantial loss

Punitive Damages
•
In addition to compensatory damages
•
Deter or punish D for misconduct
LIMITATIONS ON RECOVERY

Causation
•
Causal relationship between D’s conduct and P’s harm

Certainty
•
Prove damages with rx certainty

Duty to Mitigate
•
P has duty to rxly mitigate losses
EQUITABLE REMEDIES

Reformation
•
Re-writing of K to accurately reflect parties’ original intent

Mutual
•
Both parties mistaken about an essential element of K

Unilateral
•
One party mistaken about essential element of K

Recission
•
Cancels K due to invalid formation
•
P must
(1) Give notice, and
(2) Return benefits received from D

Specific Performance
•
D ordered to perform K
(1) Valid K exists
(2) P performed or is able to perform
(3) Monetary damages inadequate
(4) Feasibility of court’s enforcement, and
(5) No defenses exist

blocks.text:
Plaintiff’s Performance
Monetary Damages are Inadequate
•
Inadequate when (i) rare or unique property or (ii) real property

Court’s Enforcement
•
Specific performance not ordered if (i) extensive supervision or
(ii) enforcement too difficult

No Defenses
•
No specific performance if D asserts equitable defenses

Laches
(1) P unrxly delayed filing COA, and
(2) Delay causes prejudice to D

Unclean Hands
•
P engaged in unethical, illegal, or improper conduct

Injunctions
•
Court order compelling party to act or refrain from acting

Temporary Restraining Orders
•
Issued for short duration to maintain status quo
•
P must show
(1) Likely threat of irreparable harm
(2) Likely success on the merits
(3) TRO is feasible to enforce
(4) Balance of hardships in P’s favor, and
(5) No defenses

Preliminary Injunction
•
Issued before or during trial to maintain status quo
•
Notice and hearing required
•
P must show
(1) Likely threat of irreparable harm
(2) Likely success on the merits
(3) Injunction is feasible to enforce
(4) Balance of hardships in P’s favor, and
(5) No defenses

Permanent Injunction
•
Issued after P won underlying case
•
P must show
(1) Likely threat of irreparable harm
(2) Injunction feasible to enforce
(3) Balance of hardships in P’s favor, and
(4) No defenses

blocks.text:
Laches
(1) P unrxly delayed filing COA, and
(2) Delay causes prejudice to D
Unclean Hands
•
P engaged in unethical, illegal, or improper conduct
RESTITUTION REMEDIES

Legal Restitution
•
D unjustly enriched by benefit conferred by P

Money
•
If benefit is money, recovery is amount paid to D

Goods or Services
•
If benefit is goods or services, recovery based on (i) cost of providing
benefit, (ii) market value, or (iii) price specified in agreement

Replevin
•
Recover personal property wrongfully taken by D

Ejectment
•
Recover real property wrongfully possessed by D
CONTRACTS

Quasi-Contract
(1) No valid K
(2) P conferred benefit upon D
(3) P rxly expected to get paid
(4) D asked for benefit, and
(5) D unjustly enriched if allowed to retain benefit

Constructive Trust
(1) D unlawfully acquired property
(2) Would be unjustly enriched if allowed to retain, and
(3) Money damages inadequate

Tracing
•
P can reacquire through tracing
•
P entitled to appreciation in property value

Standard of Care

blocks.text:
Physicality
•
D’s physical traits taken into consideration
Intoxication
•
Voluntarily intoxicated Ds same standard as sober person
Children
•
Same standard as child of comparable age, education, intelligence,
experience
Adult Activities
•
Same standard as adult
Custom
•
Taken into consideration but not conclusive
Professionals
•
Same skill, knowledge, judgment, care as professional in good
standing within same occupation and community
Physicians
Majority Rule
•
National standard of care
Minority Rule
•
Same or similar locale
Informed Consent
•
Duty to notify patients of potential risks
•
Get informed consent prior to treatment
Negligence Per Se
(1) Statute exists imposing duty of care
(2) D violates statute
(3) P part of class the statute was created to protect
(4) D’s violation proximately causes
(5) P to suffer type of injury statute meant to prevent
Defenses
•
Rx care
•
Impossible compliance
•
Emergency

blocks.text:
•
Vagueness or ambiguity
•
Contributory negligence
•
Assumption of risk
Standards of Care for Possessors of Land
Trespassers
•
Landowner must not engage in reckless or intentional misconduct
against trespasser
Discovered
•
Obligated to warn discovered trespassers from hidden,
hazardous, and artificial conditions
Anticipated Trespassers
•
Duty owed to trespassers the landowner rxly expects
to continually enter his land
Undiscovered
•
No duty
Attractive Nuisance
•
Landowner owes duty of care to prevent fx harm to child
trespassers
•
Duty breached if
(1) Condition exists known to attract children
(2) Condition presents unrx risk of serious harm or death
(3) Children don’t realize condition’s dangerous nature
(4) Benefit of condition plus cost of remedy minimal

compared to risk, and
(5) Landowner fails to apply rx care
Invitee
•
Landowner owes duty to protect invitee from dangerous conditions
•
Duty does not extend beyond scope of invitation
Licensee
•
Landowner owed duty to remedy or warn of hidden dangers
•
No duty to examine land

                         trespassers
                  •  Duty breached if
                         (1) Condition exists known to attract children
                         (2) Condition presents unrx risk of serious harm or death
                         (3) Children don’t realize condition’s dangerous nature
                         (4) Benefit of condition plus cost of remedy minimal
                       compared to risk, and
                         (5) Landowner fails to apply rx care

    ✷  Invitee
           •  Landowner owes duty to protect invitee from dangerous conditions
           •  Duty does not extend beyond scope of invitation

    ✷  Licensee
           •  Landowner owed duty to remedy or warn of hidden dangers
           •  No duty to examine land





                          3                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                  sschimmel@swlaw.edu

blocks.text:
BREACH
Breach
•
Failure to meet applicable standard of care
Res Ipsa Loquitur
(1) Accident would not have occurred in absence of negligence
(2) D had exclusive control of instrumentality, and
(3) Injury not attributable to P

CAUSATION
Causation
Actual Cause
•
But for D’s conduct, injury would not have occurred
Proximate Cause
•
Harm was fx
Eggshell Skull
•
D takes P as he finds him
Intervening and Superseding Causes
•
Intervening: External contributing factor to P’s harm occurring after
D’s conduct
•
Superseding: Unfx intervening cause, severs D’s liability
DAMAGES
Damages
•
P must prove actual damages
•
Economic harm alone insufficient
•
Nominal damages unavailable
•
Punitive damages if D’s conduct reckless or malicious
DEFENSES
Contributory Negligence
•
P precluded from recovery if contributed to cause of injury

INTENTIONAL TORTS – HARM TO PROPERTY
Trespass to Land (transferred intent)
(1) Intentional
(2) Physical invasion of another’s land
(3) Causing
(4) Damages

Damages
•
Actual harm not required
•
Nominal damages available
Defense – Necessity
Private Necessity
•
Permitted to enter another’s land to protect own person or property
from harm
Public Necessity
•
Permitted to enter or use another’s private property to prevent
imminent harm to public
Trespass to Chattels (transferred intent)
(1) Intentional
(2) Interference with P’s chattel by dispossession or use
(3) Causing
(4) Damages
Damages
•
Compensation for loss of value or cost of repair
Conversion (transferred intent)
(1) Intentional
(2) Serious and substantial interference with P’s possession of his chattel
(3) Causing
(4) Damages

Damages
•
Full value of property at time of conversion
DEFENSES
Consent
•
Defense if voluntary

propensities

blocks.text:
Design Defect
•
Defective due to design
Consumer-Expectation Test
•
Defect rendered product more dangerous than average consumer
would rxly expect
Risk-Utility Test
•
Rx and cost-effective alternative design available to D
•
Failure to execute design → Defective and unsafe product
Failure to Warn
•
Fx risks of danger but no rx warnings

Causation
Actual Causation
•
But for defect, injury would not have occurred
Proximate Causation
•
Injury occurred while P was using product as intended
•
D still liable if P injured while misusing product if misuse fx
Damages
•
Personal injury or property damage required

Defenses
Plaintiff’s Negligence
•
Contributory negligence jx: Absolute preclusion to recovery
•
Comparative negligence jx: Recovery reduced in proportion to P’s
negligence
Assumption of Risk
•
P precluded from recovery if voluntarily assumed risk
Product Misuse or Modification
•
D not liable if misuse or modification unfx
Substantial Change
•
Substantial change to product after leaving D’s control may sever liability

Negligent Products Liability
(1) Duty

WARRANTIES
Express Warranty
•

Implied Warranty of Merchantability
•
Merchantability of goods implied in K if seller is merchant with respect to goods of
that kind
Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose
•
Seller has reason to know buyer relying on expertise to select good for particular
purpose
DEFAMATION
Defamation
(1) D’s defamatory language
(2) Of or concerning the P
(3) Publication by D to 3P, and
(4) Damage to P’s reputation

Defamatory Language
•
Negatively affects one’s reputation
•
Incites hatred, contempt, ridicule, economic injury
Of or Concerning the Plaintiff
•
Rx person would believe communication was re P
Publication
•
Communicated to 3P who comprehends nature
Republication
•
Republishers may be held liable

blocks.text:
Constitutional Requirements
Public Person
Public Official
•
High-ranking government employee
•
Running for office
Public Figure
•
Position of widespread fame or influence

Private Person and Matter of Public Concern
•
Matter of public concern: Topic of general interest to community
Falsity
•
P must prove language is false
Fault

Public Official or Public Figure
•
P must prove D’s actual malice

Private Person and Matter of Public Concern
•
P must prove D’s negligence or actual malice
Damages
Libel
•
Communicated in writing
•
Damages presumed
Slander
•
Communicated orally
•
Damages must be proved
Slander Per Se
•
Unfavorably affect P professionally
•
Assert P has loathsome disease, has committed crime of moral
turpitude, has been involved in sexual misconduct
•
Damages presumed

           •   Matter of public concern: Topic of general interest to community

    ✷  Falsity
           •  P must prove language is false

    ✷  Fault
        ✷  Public Official or Public Figure
                  •  P must prove D’s actual malice

        ✷  Private Person and Matter of Public Concern
                  •  P must prove D’s negligence or actual malice

   Damages

    ✷  Libel
           •  Communicated in writing
           •  Damages presumed

    ✷  Slander
           •  Communicated orally
           •  Damages must be proved

        ✷  Slander Per Se
                  •   Unfavorably affect P professionally
                  •   Assert P has loathsome disease, has committed crime of moral
                          turpitude, has been involved in sexual misconduct
                  •  Damages presumed

blocks.text:
Public Nuisance
•
P has right to abate by self-help
DEFENSES
Regulatory Compliance
•
Not complete defense but may be taken into consideration
Coming to the Nuisance
•
Not complete defense but may be taken into consideration
INVASION OF PRIVACY
Misappropriation
•
Unauthorized use of P’s identity for D’s commercial gain
Intrusion Upon Seclusion
(1) Prying, invading, intruding upon P’s affairs
(2) Intrusion would be highly offensive to rx person, and
(3) P’s affairs intruded upon are private
False Light
(1) D published facts about P
(2) Placing P in false light, and
(3) False light would be highly offensive to rx person
Publication of Private Facts
(1) Public disclosure of private info about P, and
(2) Disclosure would be highly offensive to rx person
MISREPRESENTATION
Intentional Misrepresentation
(1) D’s false representation of a material fact
(2) Scienter
(3) Intent to induce P’s reliance
(4) Causation
(5) Justifiable reliance, and
(6) Actual damages with possibility of consequential damages
Duty to Disclose
•
Generally, no duty to disclose

blocks.text:
Negligent Misrepresentation
(1) D’s false representation
(2) As a result of negligence
(3) Within a business transaction with P
(4) Causing
(5) P’s justifiable reliance, and
(6) Damages
MISCELLANEOUS CONCEPTS
LIABILITY
Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress
•
D causes fx harm to fx P
Zone of Danger
(1) D was negligent
(2) Placing P within zone of danger, and
(3) Threat of physical injury caused emotional distress
Bystander
•
Bystander outside zone of danger can recover if
(1) Closely related to injured person
(2) Was present where injury occurred, and
(3) Witnessed injury
Special Relationship
•
No threat of physical injury or manifestations of emotional distress required
for recovery
Vicarious Liability
Employee
•
Employer vicariously liable for employee’s torts within scope of employment
Independent Contractor
•
Employer not vicariously liable for independent contractor’s torts unless
(i) Non-delegable duty or
(ii) Inherently dangerous activity
Liability for Employer’s Own Negligence
•
Employer may be liable for negligently hiring or overseeing

blocks.text:
Multiple Parties
Joint and Several Liability
•
P can elect to recover from all Ds or a single D
Contribution
•
If P seeks recovery from single D, that D can seek contribution from other Ds
Indemnification
•
Responsibility of P’s harm shifted from one D to another
SURVIVAL ACTIONS
Survival Actions
•
Decedent’s estate representative can assert any COA that decedent could have at time
of death
Wrongful Death
•
Decedent’s spouse, kin, or representative can assert COA to recover for loss caused
by decedent’s death

Damages
•
Loss of support, companionship, and affection
•
Pain and suffering not included

blocks.text:
TRUSTS
TRUSTS
Trust
•
Trustee holds title to property for benefit of ascertainable beneficiary
Revocability
•
Revocable by default
•
Revocable trust becomes irrevocable when grantor dies or loses capacity
TYPES OF TRUSTS
EXPRESS TRUSTS
Private Express Trust
(1) Grantor with intent to create trust
(2) Trustee
(3) At least 1 ascertainable beneficiary
(4) Valid trust purpose, and
(5) Trust res
Grantor with Intent
•
Express or implied intent to create trust
Trustee
•
Person or entity holding legal title to trust res
Beneficiaries
•
Person or entity holding equitable title to trust
Trust Purpose
•
Valid if legal
Trust Res
•
Identifiable and specific trust property
Inter Vivos Trust
•
Created while grantor alive
•
Grantor has right to revoke during lifetime
Testamentary Trust
•
Created by valid will
•
Takes hold once grantor dies
•
Must be in writing
Charitable Trust
•
Created for charitable purpose that benefits society

blocks.text:
Cy Pres Doctrine
•
Substitute original purpose with another aligned with grantor’s intent
REMEDIAL TRUSTS
Resulting Trust
•
Requires trustee to revert property to grantor or his estate if trust fails
Constructive Trust
•
D holds wrongfully acquired property as trustee and must convey to P
TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
Trust Modification
Modification by Living Grantor
•
Grantor may unilaterally modify if expressly reserved right in trust
•
If failure to reserve right, grantor may still modify if
(1) All beneficiaries consent, and
(2) No interference with trust’s main purpose
Trust Termination
Expiration
•
Expires at end of stated term
Accomplished Purpose
•
Terminates once purpose is accomplished
Termination by Grantor
Revocable Trust
•
Grantor may unilaterally terminate if expressly reserved right in trust
Irrevocable Trust
•
If failure to reserve right, grantor may still terminate if all beneficiaries
consent
Termination by Beneficiaries and Trustee
•
If grantor deceased, beneficiaries may terminate if all beneficiaries and trustee
consent
Judicial Termination
•
Court may terminate if
(i) Trust purpose accomplished, or
(ii) Purpose becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible

blocks.text:
DISTRIBUTION – BENEFICIARIES AND CREDITORS
Beneficiaries
Income Beneficiary
•
Receives income from trust
Remainder Beneficiary
•
Entitled to trust principal upon termination
Discretionary Trust
•
Confers trustee with discretion to determine
•
When to pay beneficiaries
•
Which beneficiaries to pay
•
How much to pay
Mandatory Trust
•
Trustee bound by terms of trust and must distribute accordingly
Support Trust
•
Directs trustee to distribute in support of beneficiary
Spendthrift Trust
•
Confines beneficiary’s ability to alienate interest
•
Prevents creditors from reaching trust property
Alienability of Trust Property
•
Beneficiary can freely alienate interest
TRUSTEE’S POWERS, DUTIES AND REMEDIES AGAINST TRUSTEE
TRUSTEE’S POWERS
Trustee’s Powers
•
Expressly granted in trust
•
Impliedly necessary to act as rxly prudent person in directing trust
TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
Duty of Care
•
Manage trust as rxly prudent person would in managing own affairs
Duty to Prudently Invest
•
Act as rxly prudent investor would in investing own assets
Breach
•
Factors
(i) Distribution requirements
(ii) Economic condition

appreciation of capital
Duty of Impartiality
•
Remain impartial when dealing with beneficiaries
Duty of Loyalty
•
Not engage in self-dealing
•
Distribute assets in good faith and in beneficiaries’ interest
Duty to Disclose
•
Fully disclose truthful info about status of assets
Duty to Account
•
Account for actions made in furtherance of trust
Delegation of Duties
•
Common law: Prohibited from delegating authority
•

•

•
Fail to avert breach, or
•
Wrongfully delegate authority
Third-Party Liability
•
3P liable for losses if knowingly takes part in breach

blocks.text:
Attested Will
(1) Writing
(2) Signed
(3) By (i) the testator or (ii) another person at the testator’s direction and in his presence
(4) 2 witnesses
(5) Witnesses required to sign during testator’s life, and
(6) Witnesses must know document is a will

Interested Witnesses
•
If interested witness, 2 disinterested witnesses must be present

Substantial Compliance
•
Strict compliance not required
•
May still be valid if proponent can show testator intended document to be will

Holographic Will
(1) Signed by testator
(2) Testamentary intent, and
(3) Material provisions in testator’s handwriting

Lack of Date
•
Not invalidated for lack of date

Codicil
•
Will supplement
•
Must satisfy will formalities
•
Republishes will as to date of codicil

Choice of Law
•
Valid will complies with
•
CA required formalities
•
Formalities of testator’s domicile
•
Formalities of state where will executed

Capacity
•
Legal age
•
Adequate mental capacity
•
Nature of act
•
Nature and scope of property
•
Natural objects of bounty

WILLS

VALIDITY OF WILLS

     EXECUTION OF WILLS

      ✷   Attested Will
                 (1) Writing
                 (2) Signed
                 (3) By (i) the testator or (ii) another person at the testator’s direction and in his presence
                 (4) 2 witnesses
                 (5) Witnesses required to sign during testator’s life, and
                 (6) Witnesses must know document is a will

          ✷  Interested Witnesses
                     •    If interested witness, 2 disinterested witnesses must be present

      ✷   Substantial Compliance
              •   Strict compliance not required
              •  May still be valid if proponent can show testator intended document to be will

      ✷  Holographic Will
                 (1) Signed by testator
                 (2) Testamentary intent, and
                 (3) Material provisions in testator’s handwriting

          ✷  Lack of Date
                     •  Not invalidated for lack of date

      ✷   Codicil
              •   Will supplement
              •  Must satisfy will formalities
              •   Republishes will as to date of codicil

      ✷  Choice of Law
              •   Valid will complies with
                  •  CA required formalities
                  •   Formalities of testator’s domicile
                  •   Formalities of state where will executed

     CHALLENGES TO VALIDITY

      ✷  Capacity
              •   Legal age
              •  Adequate mental capacity
              •   Nature of act
              •   Nature and scope of property
              •   Natural objects of bounty

blocks.text:
Insane Delusion
• Irrational belief not supported by facts
• May invalidate will
Undue Influence
•
Coercion or excessive influence such that testator lacks free will
•
May invalidate will
•
Factors
(i) Testator’s vulnerability
(ii) Influencer’s apparent authority
(iii) Influencer’s actions and tactics
(iv) Equity of result

Confidential Relationship
•
Creates presumption of influence
(1) Confidential relationship between testator and principal beneficiary
(2) Beneficiary active in procuring will, and
(3) Devise to beneficiary is unnatural
Susceptibility Test
(1) Testator was susceptible
(2) Influence had motive
(3) Influencer had opportunity, and
(4) Testator affected unnatural outcome
Fraud

Fraud in the Inducement
•
Intentional misrepresentation → Testator procures will she would not have
otherwise procured

Fraud in the Execution
•
Misrepresentation re type of document or contents → Testator unknowingly
creates will
REVOCATION OF WILLS

Revocation by Physical Act
•
By testator or another in testator’s presence
•
Burning, tearing, canceling, destroying, crossing out, obliterating
•
With testator’s intent to revoke

Lost Wills are Presumed Revoked
•
Lost will creates rebuttable presumption that testator revoked will by physical act

Revocation by Subsequent Instrument
•
Revocation through valid subsequent will or codicil

blocks.text:
Revocation by Operation of Law
•
Divorce or dissolution of domestic partnership revokes provisions benefiting former spouse
or domestic partner

Revocation of Codicil
•
Revocation of will revokes all codicils
•
Revocation of codicil revives underlying will
REVIVAL OF REVOKED WILLS

Revival of Will or Codicil Revoked by Physical Act
•
Can be revived if testator had intent to revive

Dependent Relative Revocation
•
Applicable when testator ineffectively revokes will due to mistaken belief of law or fact
TERMS AND COMPONENTS OF WILLS

CONSTRUCTION AND INTERPRETATION
Integration
•
All documents present at execution integrated into will
•
Testator’s intent required
Acts of Independent Significance
• Events holding significance outside will
Incorporation by Reference
(1) Testator intended document to be included in will
(2) Document existed when will executed, and
(3) Document described with sufficient certainty
Ambiguities
•
Extrinsic evidence admissible to resolve
DISPOSITION
Classification
Specific Gift
•
Specifically identified in will
General Gift
•
Nonspecific devise satisfied from general assets
Demonstrative Gift
•
General gift identifying source to satisfy gift
Residuary Gift
•
Any property remaining

blocks.text:
Ademption
Ademption by Extinction
•
If specific gift not in testator’s estate upon death, gift adeems
Ademption by Satisfaction
•
Testator satisfies gift through inter vivos transfer
Abatement
•
Reduction of testamentary gifts to pay debts when assets insufficient
•
Abatement order
(1) Intestate property
(2) Residuary gifts
(3) General gifts to nonrelatives
(4) General gifts to relatives
(5) Specific gifts to nonrelatives
(6) Specific gifts to relatives
Lapse and Anti-Lapse
Lapse
•
Common law: Gift lapsed if beneficiary predeceased testator
Exception – Class Gifts
•
If class member predeceases testator, other class members share
deceased member’s share
Anti-Lapse
•
No lapse if beneficiary
(1) Blood relative of testator or of her current spouse or domestic partner,

and
(2) Predeceased testator and left issue
Class Gifts
•
If class and anti-lapse inapplicable, predeceased member’s issue
takes gift
BARS TO SUCCESSION
The Slayer Rule
•
Murderer cannot inherit from victim
•
Anti-lapse inapplicable
Elder Abuse
•
Beneficiary who commits elder abuse cannot inherit from victim

blocks.text:
Disclaimer
(1) Signed
(2) Written Disclaimer
(3) Identifies decedent, disclaimed devise, and extent of disclaimer, and
(4) Within 9 months of testator’s death
Disinherited Children
• Parent may disinherit child through valid will
INTESTACY
Intestate Succession
•
Absent valid will, estate distributed through intestacy
Intestate Distribution Order
(1)
Decedent’s issue
(2)
If no issue, to parent(s)
(3)
If none of the above, to issue of parent(s)
(4)
If none of the above, to grandparent(s)
(5)
If none of the above, to issue of grandparent(s)
(6)
If none of the above, to issue of predeceased spouse or domestic partner
(7)
If none of the above, decedent’s next of kin
(8)
If none of the above, to parent(s) of predeceased spouse or domestic partner
(9)
If none of the above, to issue of parent(s) of predeceased spouse or domestic
partner
(10) If none of the above, the estate escheats to the state
120-Hour Rule
•
Heir must show she survived decedent by 120 hours
•
Otherwise, treated as if predeceased decedent

INTESTATE DISTRIBUTION
Surviving Spouse
Community Property
•
Entitled to half of CP and QCP
Separate Property
•
Contingent upon number of surviving relatives
Spouse Only
•
Receives 100%
Spouse + 1
•
50/50 split between surviving spouse and other relative
Spouse + 2
•
Receives 1/3

blocks.text:
Issue
•
Decedent’s lineal descendants
Calculating Share
If Equal Degree of Kinship – Per Capita
•
Equal distribution of intestate property
If Unequal Degree of Kinship – Per Capita with Representation
•
Equal division of intestate property among first level of decedent’s
surviving descendants
•
If deceased member left issue → Issue takes
•
If deceased member has no surviving issue → Member does not take

Advancements
Common Law
•
Any inter vivos gift → Rebuttable presumption gift was
advancement
Modern Law
•
Treated as advancement if gift stated or acknowledged in writing as
advancement

Hotchpot
(1) Add the value of the advancement back into the intestate estate
(2) Divide the resulting estate by the number of taking heirs
(3) Deduct the heir’s advancement from the heir’s intestate share

OTHER INTESTATE ISSUES
Omitted Spouse
•
Surviving spouse omitted from will executed prior to marriage to testator
•
Entitled to intestate share
Distribution
•
Entitled to
•
Half of decedent’s CP and QCP, and
•
Share of decedent’s SP equal to share through intestacy
Children
Omitted Children
•
Entitled to intestate share if
•
Will created prior to birth or adoption, or
•
Mistaken belief child was dead or nonexistent
Limitation
•
Not entitled to intestate share if
•
Intentionally omitted

                         (3) Deduct the heir’s advancement from the heir’s intestate share

OTHER INTESTATE ISSUES

  ✷  Omitted Spouse
       •   Surviving spouse omitted from will executed prior to marriage to testator
       •   Entitled to intestate share

      ✷  Distribution
              •   Entitled to
                  •   Half of decedent’s CP and QCP, and
                  •   Share of decedent’s SP equal to share through intestacy

       Children

      ✷  Omitted Children
              •   Entitled to intestate share if
                  •   Will created prior to birth or adoption, or
                  •   Mistaken belief child was dead or nonexistent

          ✷  Limitation
                     •  Not entitled to intestate share if
                         •   Intentionally omitted

                                   6                                           Prof. Sarah Schimmel
                                                                              sschimmel@swlaw.edu
pages.raw_text:
Issue
       •   Decedent’s lineal descendants

      ✷  Calculating Share

          ✷   If Equal Degree of Kinship – Per Capita
                     •   Equal distribution of intestate property

          ✷   If Unequal Degree of Kinship – Per Capita with Representation
                     •   Equal division of intestate property among first level of decedent’s
                           surviving descendants
                     •    If deceased member left issue → Issue takes
                     •    If deceased member has no surviving issue → Member does not take

             Advancements

                Common Law
                     •  Any inter vivos gift → Rebuttable presumption gift was
                        advancement

                   Modern Law
                     •   Treated as advancement if gift stated or acknowledged in writing as
                        advancement

                     Hotchpot
                         (1) Add the value of the advancement back into the intestate estate
                         (2) Divide the resulting estate by the number of taking heirs
                         (3) Deduct the heir’s advancement from the heir’s intestate share

OTHER INTESTATE ISSUES

  ✷  Omitted Spouse
       •   Surviving spouse omitted from will executed prior to marriage to testator
       •   Entitled to intestate share

      ✷  Distribution
              •   Entitled to
                  •   Half of decedent’s CP and QCP, and
                  •   Share of decedent’s SP equal to share through intestacy

       Children

      ✷  Omitted Children
              •   Entitled to intestate share if
                  •   Will created prior to birth or adoption, or
                  •   Mistaken belief child was dead or nonexistent

          ✷  Limitation
                     •  Not entitled to intestate share if
                         •   Intentionally omitted
